alex_hormozi.specialty: Offers, scaling, business acquisition, value equations

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Unknown Talent Ratio
emails.body:
Words I like: Hiring talented people can be one of the easiest 10x-100x ROI opportunities. And there's always underpriced talent.

Mozi Minute: LTGPE:CAT

Hypothetically, would you pay $50,000 to make $400,000?

Of course. It's an 8x return.

But most business owners won't do this when it comes to hiring.

They'll complain about not being able to find talent.

They'll say "good people don't exist anymore."

But they're just not paying enough.

Example:

A business needs a 6 figure role.

Someone who would make them multiple six figures in profit.

Every year.

But they only spend a few thousand dollars and make an indeed post to hire this person.

People will spend $10,000 on Facebook ads to get customers at a 3x return.

But won't spend $10,000 to find someone who's going to make them $100,000 every year.

The problem is most people have no idea what an employee is actually worth to them.

So they do what everyone else does.

And what everyone else does doesn't work.

That's why everyone complains about hiring.

Here's the fix:

LTGPE:CAT

Lifetime Gross Profit Per Employee : Cost to Acquire Talent

This equation tells you exactly how much an employee is worth.

Once you know that number, you can spend the right amount to get them.

How to calculate it:

- Figure out how much gross profit a role brings into your business every year.
- Subtract what you pay that person.

That's your gross profit per employee per year.

Now if you knew you'd make that much profit, how much would you be willing to spend to get it?

That's your cost to acquire talent.

Hypothetical Example:

Let's say you own a dental practice.

You need to hire a doctor.

This doctor can do $500,000 worth of work every year.

And you pay the doctor $100,000 annually.

So every year, this one employee makes you $400,000 in gross profit.

Your equation is: $400,000 : whatever you're willing to spend

If someone walked up to you and said...

"Give me $50,000 and I'll give you back $400,000 in twelve months"

Would you do it?

Of course.

That's an 8:1 return. In year one.

But most dental practices?

They're spending maybe $5,000 to hire a doctor.

That's why they can't find talent.

Proof:

A friend of mine owns an insurance agency.

He was struggling to hire sales reps.

And he was paying a $500 bonus to his employee to incentivize referrals.

No one gave any.

Once he figured out that each rep made him $150,000 in gross profit in 6 months.

He changed his bonus for his employees from $500 to $25,000.

Guess what happened?

He no longer had hiring issues.

Why this works:

Good people exist.

But good people cost money to find.

You can't get a 10x return by spending like everyone else.

Everyone else is broke. Everyone else can't find talent.

You don't want to do what they do.

You want to spend more than everyone else.

Because when you spend more, you get more.

You get better recruiters. You get better job posts. You get better referrals.

You get access to people who aren't even looking for jobs.

You just have to be willing to make the trade.

As promised, value under a minute.

Pay more for talent,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Make Hooks That Convert
emails.body:
Words I like: Make hooks specific enough to get the right people and broad enough to get as many of them as you can.

Mozi Minute: Make Hooks that Convert

Most people obsess over tweaking their ad script, offer, or CTA.

That's why their ads don't work.

People decide if they're going to watch your ad in the first 3 seconds.

If the hook sucks, nothing else matters.

Here's the kicker:

A strong hook can 2-3x the number of people who keep watching.

Meaning you can 2-3x sales by changing 3 seconds of your ad. Not bad.

Here's my Hook Checklist:

First Time Through:
- Look at top-performing ads or content of other people
- Isolate the hooks
- Write down your favorite 50
- Use all of them in ads and content

Every Time After:
- Look at top 10% performers (top 5)
- Make 70% of your next batch of ads using those top 5 hooks
- Make 20% doing permutations of those hooks (think both visuals and words)
- Make 10% completely different hooks

Good Hooks Contain:
- Some way to catch their attention (call out)
- Some implication of value if they consume it

Real examples of my top performing hooks:

Ads:
- Real quick question...can I have your email address?
- The rumors are true...
- Read this if you want to win.

YT:
- You guys want to hear something completely insane
- On November 30th, 2022 the world changed forever.

IG:
- If you want someone to treat you differently, you have to address it as soon as possible
- Here's a sign that you're hiring dumb people
- If you have no money you start by selling your time, because you have time.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Write better hooks.

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Why You Should Sell 1-on-1 Time
emails.body:
Words I like: "This is America, everything is for sale!"

Mozi Minute: Why You Should Sell 1-on-1 Time

Most people think selling your time 1-on-1 is unscalable and beneath them.

They're wrong.

It's actually the fastest way to get started and the highest margin business you can run.

The Core Principle:

Either sell extremely expensive stuff to a select few OR sell super cheap to everyone.

The middle is where businesses die.

When starting out, go expensive.

Tesla started with a $250,000 Roadster, then worked down to the Model 3.

Same strategy works for you.

Why Sell Your Time:

- You Learn Faster
- Wealthy People Shift Your Beliefs
- Everyone Already Sells Their Time
- You Keep 100% Margin
- It Anchors Your Other Offers

Money Math:

Let's say 90 people buy at $100 and 10 people buy at $1,000.

If you make 40% margin on the $100 tier:
- 90 x $100 x 40% = $3,600 profit

If you make 100% margin on the $1,000 tier:
- 10 x $1,000 x 100% = $10,000 profit

Result: The expensive tier with only 10% of customers generates 75% of your profit.

How To Create Your Expensive Offer:

Framework 1: 10-100x Your Current Price
Framework 2: Word of Mouth Only
Framework 3: Remove Unscalable, 10x Value

Implementation:

List your premium offer wherever you sell.

When you present it, confront the high price head-on:

"Working with me 1-on-1 is $10,000. This is the fastest way to get you where you want to go."

Then shut up. Let them respond.

Bottom Line:

Every business can have 5 premium 1-on-1 clients.

Cap the supply. Charge accordingly.

Crush it.

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: My Favorite Upsell Of All Time
emails.body:
Words I like: If you make it easy for people to buy, more people will.

Mozi Minute: My favorite upsell of all time

Most business owners try to upsell by asking, "Do you want the premium version?"

This gives an easy out. And invites objections.

My favorite upsell of all time is the menu upsell. Here's why:

- It removes the yes/no decision
- You don't pitch, you prescribe
- The close is frictionless

How it works:

- Unsell what they don't need. This builds instant trust and reciprocity.
- Prescribe what they do need. Tell them what they need with detailed personalized instruction.
- Ask if they prefer A or B (nudge to one option).
- Make it easy to pay. My favorite: "card on file?"

Example (supplements):

- Unsell - "Since you're trying to lose weight you don't need the mass gainer or testosterone booster."
- Prescribe - "But you will need the pre workout. Take this everyday 30 minutes before heading to the gym."
- A/B - "For the pre workout did you want cherry or lime? I prefer cherry."
- Easy to pay - "Did you just want to use the card on file for that?"

As promised, value in under a minute.

Talk soon,

- Alex

emails.body:

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: One Extra Billing Cycle Per Year
emails.body:
Words I like: When push comes to shove...Cashflow > Profit > Revenue

Mozi minute: An Extra Billing Cycle Per Year

If you run a recurring business.

Bill every 4 weeks instead of every month.

It is a great way to increase profit.

In a year there are 13, four-week cycles.

Compared to 12, one-month cycles.

Meaning you get an extra billing cycle with no extra work.

Front end sales hardly dip from billing every 28 days.

It feels the same as monthly to customers.

But, this creates an 8.3% increase in revenue that drops disproportionately to the bottom line.

Hypothetical example:

Let's say you have 600 customers paying $100 per month.

That's $1,200 per year each.

At a 20% margin, you make $240 in profit per customer.

In total you're doing $720,000 in revenue and $144,000 in profit.

But...

If you bill $100 every 4 weeks instead.

Customers now pay about $1,300 per year at $340 profit.

Total revenue climbs to $780,000.

Profit sits at $204,000 (if fixed costs stay the same.)

Same customers. Same work. $60,000 more profit.

Proof:

We used this at over 5,000 gyms at GymLaunch and saw zero effects in sales.

Most gyms not only had an 8% increase in revenue but significantly greater increases in profit.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Bill every 4 weeks instead of monthly,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Scaling Your Sales Script
emails.body:
Words I like: The money is in the maybes

Mozi Minute: Build a Sales Script that Scales

Every interaction with a customer should be scripted. Especially sales. No script means no process.

No process means:
- Reps say whatever they want
- Training becomes complex
- Bad expectations get set
- Compliance drops
- You lose sales

That's why every sales conversation in our portfolio follows a script.

Here's how to make one:

- Do - Have the founder or sales leader take 50+ calls. They sell the best so we should replicate what they say.

- Document - Use all 50 call recordings to create a rough draft for a script.

Organize the script into the CLOSER framework:
- Clarify why they're on the call
- Label them with a problem
- Overview their past experiences (Dig into the problem to create pain)
- Sell the vacation (Pitch the outcome, not the product)
- Explain away their beliefs (Handle objections)
- Reinforce the decision

- Delete - Simple and short wins. Delete as much as possible that won't affect close rate. Under 500 words (4 minutes of talking). Make it a 3rd grade reading level.

- Delegate - Delegate the script to the team. Now it's all about script adherence.

Why does this work?

Most business owners treat sales like an art. That's why they get inconsistent results.

Sales isn't art. It's a process that can be operationalized.

A script is words that if said in a specific order increase the likelihood someone buys.

This causes:
- Reps to ramp up faster
- Close rates become predictable
- Training becomes simple

Use a script,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Angry Boat
emails.body:
When I was 18, I worked for a fur coat dealer.

One day, a lady storms in, furious about a missing button on her expensive coat.

The owner rolled his eyes towards me in the back of the store, out of eyesight.

He got up, then transformed his face as he rounded the corner to the retail area.

He took it up a notch "THAT'S RIDICULOUS! Who sold you this coat?"

He grabbed it from her to inspect the grace indiscretion.

"Give me their name. I'll get to the bottom of this right away. They're going to be held accountable for this! Did anyone see you in it? This is completely unacceptable."

Suddenly, she's backpedaling:

"Well, it's not THAT bad..."

He fired back.

"It is that bad. We'll get this taken care of right away."

He handed it to the seamstress in the back.

She sewed the button on in minutes.

He chit chatted and handed her back her coat within a few minutes.

"Of course, this is on the house. My deepest apologies. Ridiculous."

"Oh you're the best thank you for always looking out for us."

She'd completely changed her tune.

She left. The door closed. As soon as she left he grinned at me.

"There can only be one person in the angry boat."

This taught me the most powerful customer service lesson of my life.

How to deal with angry customers:

Step 1: Match Their Energy (Plus 20%)
Step 2: Direct Anger at the Problem (Not Them)
Step 3: Take Immediate Dramatic Action
Step 4: Watch Them Calm Down

They came ready to fight. But you're on their side, fighting harder than they are.

There's no one left to fight.

I've trained every support team I've ever had on the angry boat since.

It works every time.

The customer who was about to leave a one-star review becomes your biggest advocate.

Try it next time and let me know how it goes,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Big Head, Long Tail
emails.body:
Words I like: Motivation has an expiration date. Offer the most when motivation is highest.

Mozi Minute: Big Head, Long Tail

Many businesses offer trials and low-ticket offers. This is fine if your goal is automated conversion.

But if you have a sales person - which 80% of you do...

Do not start with a trial.

Your prospects are most excited about solving their problem the moment they decide to fix it.

That's when you should charge the most.

The Big Head, Long Tail Model:

Charge a lot upfront when motivation peaks. Then add lower-priced continuity on the backend. Big purchase happens when pain is highest. Recurring revenue comes after they're committed.

Why This Crushes Trials:

- Offsets CAC Immediately
- Captures Peak Motivation
- Gets Them More Invested
- Screens For Better Customers
- Higher LTV, Lower Churn

Real Example:

My gym business sold six-week challenges for $500-$600 upfront. Not trials. Not monthly memberships. Full transformation packages with refundable deposits if they hit their goals.

This meant I could spend $200-$300 on ads and still be profitable immediately.

Competitors were bleeding cash waiting for monthly payments to cover their ad spend.

I outspent everyone, ate up marketshare, and stayed profitable.

Bottom Line: When someone has real urgency, sell them the complete solution at a premium price. Save the continuity for after they've gotten results.

Sell the cure, not the band-aid,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Active Beats Passive
emails.body:
Words I like: The market gives you 10% returns. Your business probably gives you more.

Mozi Money Minute: Active Beats Passive

People will spend 100 hours trying to make $100/mo passive when they could've made $10,000 getting paid for their active time.

Bad trade.

What everyone seems to forget is that the people with passive income often had massive active income first.

Here's some math that shifted my perspective:

Stock market historically returns ~10% annually.

Small businesses on the other hand can 10x in a year. That's 1000%.

Illustration:

Fishing company. $100k to invest. Two options:

- Pay taxes. Now invest $60k into the market and make $6k per year.
- Buy a boat: Pay no taxes. Boat makes $100k next year with 40% margins = $40k per year.

He buys the boat. Better decision.

Active income investing thinking process:

- Reinvest where you have control
- Stack active income first
- Think equipment/talent/marketing, not equities

Your business is the asset. Everything else is the hedge.

$100k to $1M happens all the time in business. In the market? That's a decade.

Talk soon,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: How To Ask For Referrals on Sales Calls
emails.body:
Words I like: Nothing will net you more money per second than asking for referrals on sales calls.

I am going to show you exactly how to do it in 4 steps.

Step 1: Compliment
Find something genuine to compliment them on. This isn't sycophantic. It's real. When they get the referral call, they will have told that person a good thing about you. Your social proof will precede you.

Step 2: Get an Introduction
Ask them directly to introduce you. (Not for a referral to call, but an actual introduction from them).

Step 3: Give them words to use
Tell them exactly what to say when they introduce you. This is critical. Most people make this mistake. They ask for referrals but don't give the words to use.

Step 4: Get More
Ask "who else" right after you close one. This plants a seed for the next referral.

Over 30% of my sales came from referrals.

I spent maybe 1 minute per referral ask.

Do the math.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Lifestyle Risk vs Business Risk
emails.body:
Words I like: Poor people are impressed by stuff. Rich people are impressed by results.

Here's a meta insight:

You can't take maximum risk in both your lifestyle AND your business at the same time.

You have to pick one.

Elon doesn't have a fancy house. He sleeps at the factory. His lifestyle risk is low. His business risk is maxed.

Sam Walton drove a beat-up truck and lived in a modest home in Arkansas. His lifestyle risk was low. His business risk was maxed.

Most broke people do the opposite. They max out their lifestyle (big house, nice car, expensive habits) and then take zero business risk because they can't afford to fail.

Keep your lifestyle lean.

Maximize your business risk.

That's how you win.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: How To Open A Sales Call
emails.body:
Words I like: You can't close what you do not open.

Most people fumble the open.

Here's how to do it:

The Big Four (in this order):
1. Name - Give your name
2. Company - Give your company name
3. Recorded - Ask if the call is being recorded (legal and relationship builder)
4. Reason - Give the reason for the call in 1 sentence

Then:

State the Agenda - Tell them what will happen on the call.

Example:

"Hi, this is Alex with Acquisition. Just so you know, I'm recording this call for quality purposes, is that cool? Great. I'm calling because [1 sentence reason]. What I'd like to do is ask you a few questions, see if there's a fit, and if there is, we can talk about next steps. Sound good?"

Notice:
- It's not salesy
- It's direct
- They know what to expect
- You set the frame

That's how you open a call.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Mozi Minute: 70% Calendar Rule of Thumb
emails.body:
Words I like: You can't beat someone who has already decided.

Here's a useful heuristic for sales team management:

The sweet spot for calendar utilization is about 70%.

If reps are at 100% calendar utilization, conversion rates drop because they don't have time to prepare, and they get burnt out.

If reps are at 30% calendar utilization, they get the "commission breath" (panicked selling) and aren't paced for success.

70% is the sweet spot.

Here's the math:

5 reps at 100% calendar utilization closing at 20% = 100 deals
7 reps at 70% calendar utilization closing at 30% = 147 deals

The second team closes 47% more deals with better quality conversations and happier reps.

You're welcome.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Risk Reversal Gold
emails.body:
Words I like: Opportunity only looks like opportunity looking backwards.

Most people think risk reversal means just offering a guarantee.

But there's a deeper level.

Think like an insurance model.

Recruiters: "We replace any bad hire for free for the first 90 days."

Agencies: "We extend your campaign for free until you hit ROI."

Therapists: "We offer a free session to see if we're a good fit."

Software: "You don't pay until implementation is complete."

The pattern: Make it so the customer has ZERO downside risk, and YOU take the risk.

People will pay more and longer to avoid pain than they will for pleasure.

Use this.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Show Rate Hack
emails.body:
Words I like: More potential is wasted through inaction than incompetence.

Here's a simple hack to increase show rates:

When a prospect self-schedules (books their own call), call them right then and offer to slide them up to a sooner time.

I call this the "Pull Forward Technique."

Benefits:

1. Same-day show rates are higher than booked-future show rates
2. Fewer blank spaces in your calendar
3. Close hot leads immediately
4. Opens up future slots for new calls
5. Disqualify faster on bad fits
6. Creates momentum for your sales team

It's a small thing that changes the entire game.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Sell The Prospect Not The Product
emails.body:
Words I like: If you can articulate someone's pain better than they can, they assume you have the cure.

Here's a principle that will cut your CAC in half:

80% of the conversation should be about THEM.
20% of the conversation should be about your PRODUCT.

Most people do the opposite.

Example:
Prospect is overweight.
Don't talk about your meal plan.
Talk about their pain.

"So walk me through a day in your life. What's it like not having the energy to play with your kids? What do you think that costs you emotionally? What about your health? What are you worried about 5 years from now?"

Once they've articulated their pain deeply enough, mentioning your solution feels like a relief.

Pain is the pitch.

Sell the prospect, not the product.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Memorize Scripts In 48 Hours
emails.body:
Words I like: Most people think that hard part is getting started. The hard part is staying consistent.

Here's how to memorize scripts in 48 hours:

The Black-Out Method:

1. Print out your script (should be under 500 words, roughly 4 minutes of talking)
2. Print two copies
3. Read through once
4. Black out every 5th word on the second copy
5. Read it again, filling in the blanks
6. Black out more words
7. Repeat until you can recite it from memory

The key is FORCED RETRIEVAL.

Your brain doesn't memorize well from passive reading.

But when you force your brain to retrieve information, it sticks.

Try it.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Every Business Has A Shape
emails.body:
Words I like: There's really only one belief you need to win. That your situation is changeable.

Every business has a shape.

Let me show you the 4 main ones:

1. Ecommerce Shape = Staircase
You climb up, but it's not exponential. It's linear steps. Because inventory limits you.

2. Service Shape = Steady Line
You grow linearly because you're limited by people and time. Can't leverage.

3. Info Shape = Fast Curve Then Plateau
Rapid growth early (viral, word of mouth) then hits ceiling because there's a market cap for your audience.

4. Software Shape = Dud Then Explode
Slow early (build moat, compound value) then exponential later (leverage, recurring revenue, infinite scaling).

Know your shape.

Align your goals to your shape.

That's how you set realistic expectations.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Pulling Teeth
emails.body:
Words I like: Pain motivates purchases better than pressure.

Here's a discovery technique I use:

When someone gives you a vague answer, don't move on.

Pull the teeth.

Use two magic questions:

1. "Can you tell me more about that?"
2. "Can you give me an example?"

There are three types of bad answers:

1. Vague (not specific)
2. Confusing (unclear)
3. Incomplete (missing critical details)

Keep pulling teeth until you get specific, painful details.

Example:
Them: "We're not getting enough leads."
You: "Can you tell me more about that?"
Them: "Well, our lead generation is weak."
You: "Can you give me an example?"
Them: "We're only getting 5 leads per month and we need 20."
You: "How is that impacting your business?"
Them: "We're losing $50k per month in revenue."

Now you have the REAL problem.

Pull teeth in discovery.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Entrepreneur Math
emails.body:
Words I like: Your company's revenue ceiling is determined by the collective skills of the people contained within it.

Mozi Minute: Entrepreneur Math

Most business owners think they need "employees."

Wrong.

You need entrepreneurs at every level.

Here's the math:

• A $1M business = 1 $1M entrepreneur with 2-3 $100k+ entrepreneurs working under them.

• An $10M business = 1 $10M entrepreneur with 2-3 $1M+ entrepreneurs working under them.

• A $100M business = 1 nine-figure entrepreneur with many $10M+ entrepreneurs working under them.

The Pattern:

Your company is just a stack of entrepreneurs at different levels.

The top person sets the ceiling. The people under them determine if you hit it.

Why This Matters:

If your company has MORE entrepreneurial talent than your current revenue level, you're about to grow.

If it has LESS, you're about to plateau or decline.

Example 1:

You're doing $2M with a team of people who could each run $500k businesses on their own.

You have $2M+ of entrepreneurial horsepower under you.

You're about to explode.

Example 2:

You're doing $5M with a team of people who've never run anything beyond $100k.

You have maybe $500k of entrepreneurial horsepower total. You're maxed out.

Growth will be painful until you upgrade the team.

The Diagnostic:

Look at your leadership team.

Could each of them run a business at the level right below yours?

If you're doing $10M, could your VPs each run $1-3M businesses on their own?

If not, you don't have enough horsepower to scale.

Real World Example:

When Gym Launch was doing $10M, I had people on the team who could easily run $1M+ businesses. That's why we hit $30M+ so fast.

The talent was already there. We just had to give them the systems and get out of the way.

When we tried to go from $25M to $50M, we didn't have enough eight-figure entrepreneurs on the team yet. We plateaued. Hard.

We had to upgrade before we could grow again.

The biggest person who was constrained was me - I thought being a $100M entrepreneur meant I needed to level up - when in reality - I had been afraid of paying great people big amounts of money to get the whole company to the next level.

Bottom Line:

Count the entrepreneurial horsepower on your team.

If it's more than your current revenue, you're about to grow.

If it's less, you're about to plateau. Upgrade accordingly.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Hire up or plateau,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The #1 Thing
emails.body:
Words I like: The biggest threat to your business isn't doing nothing.

It's doing the second most important thing.

The things that you can reasonably argue is a good idea to anyone - except it isn't the BEST return on time.

It gives you returns. You grow by 20-30%, but there's a 2-3-5-10x sitting there - it's just harder than the incremental move.

Mozi Minute: The #1 Thing

Most businesses fail because they work on the wrong stuff.

Not because they don't work hard.

This is mostly because the hardest problems are ones that usually you don't know how to solve yet - and neither does anyone else on your team.

So you focus on the ones you do know how to solve - rather than beginning on the unknown path.

Interesting Case Study:

A new venture firm called HF0 gets 3x better returns than Y Combinator.

How?

They lock founders in a basement for 12 straight weeks and force them to work on ONE thing - their #1 TRUE constraint.

And they observe them working to prevent them from going off task.

For 12. Straight. Weeks.

No context switching. No notifications. Just brutal effort on the one problem.

Why Second-Best Problems Are Deadly:

Working on your second-best problem is reasonable. No one will blame you.

But when you work on second-best problems, you get second best problem returns.

These are typically incremental improvements not orders of magnitude.

And that's where the real value lies.

Example:

Your real problem is you need to figure out a way to reliably attract talent for a role that you have a lot of (technicians, account managers, etc).

But instead you work on your sales motion, more ads, slack messages, and everything else that's not that.

How To Find Your #1 Thing:

Ask yourself:

"If we could only work on ONE thing for the next 90 days that 2-3x'd the business, what would it be?"

Not two things. One.

If you can't answer, answering that question becomes your first priority.

Common #1 constraints by stage:

$0-$100k - You don't have an offer people want
$100k-$500k - You can't generate enough leads
$500k-$1M - You can't close leads well enough
$1M-$3M - You can't deliver without doing everything yourself
$3M-$10M - You don't have the right people in key roles
$10M-30M - Your systems can't handle more volume
$30M+ - Need entrepreneurial level leaders with strong incentives by division

Bottom Line:

Anything that isn't your #1 constraint is stealing time from what actually moves the needle.

Pick one. Go all in. Fix it. Then pick the next one.

As promised, value in under a minute.

One thing. All in.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: 5 Ways To Expand Your Niche
emails.body:
Words I like: The key to being fearless is having nothing to lose. And the only way to have nothing to lose is to realize nothing in the world belonged to you to begin with.

Mozi Minute: 5 Ways To Expand Your Niche

Most businesses hit a revenue ceiling and panic.

They think they've maxed out their market.

They haven't. They just don't know the five ways to reposition.

Here they are:

1. Up Market (Fewer Bigger Customers, Fewer Bigger Checks)

Go after larger customers who pay more. Same service, higher price, bigger scale.

Example:

Marketing agency goes from $2k/mo small businesses to $20k/mo mid-market companies.

Revenue per client goes 10x.

You need fewer clients to hit the same revenue.

Less chaos, more profit.

The catch:

Bigger customers demand more proof, longer sales cycles, and better delivery. But the math works if you can handle it.

2. Down Market (More Smaller Customers, Simpler Offers)

Serve smaller customers with stripped-down, cheaper versions of what you do.

Example:

Business coach charging $10k drops to $997 for a course version.

Same core content, self-service delivery.

You trade margin per customer for volume. Works when you can automate delivery.

The catch:

You need way more customers to hit the same revenue. Only works if you can scale without adding proportional costs.

3. Adjacent Market (Same Size, But Different Avatar, Result = More Customers Same Ticket Size)

Find customers who look almost identical to your current ones but live in a slightly different world.

Example:

You sell HR software to law firms. Adjacent move? Accounting firms.

Same pain points, same buying behavior, different industry terminology.

The catch:

You already have proof of concept. Your offer works.

You just need to translate it to the new avatar's language and context.

4. Broader Market (Widen The Category)

Expand to serve the entire category instead of just one slice.

Example:

You help CrossFit gyms get leads.

Broader move? Help ALL fitness businesses get leads.

Gyms, yoga studios, martial arts, personal trainers.

Or even wider - all health and wellness.

The catch:

You lose some relevance and specificity. Your messaging becomes less targeted.

You might need different case studies for different sub-segments.

But your addressable market grows.

5. Narrower Market (Niche Down For Premium)

Go smaller and more specific to charge more and win faster.

Example:

Instead of "marketing for dentists," become "marketing for cosmetic dentists in cities over 500k population."

You become THE expert. You charge 3x more. Close rates double because your relevance is on point.

The catch:

Your market shrinks. But if the niche is big enough and you can win it, the premium pricing and efficiency more than make up for it.

How To Pick Your Path:

Ask yourself three questions:

- Where's my constraint?

If it's delivery capacity, go up market or narrower. If it's lead volume, go broader or adjacent.

- What's my proof?

More proof = easier to go up market. Less proof = go narrower to build it faster.

- What's my tolerance?

More customers = more chaos. Bigger customers = more complexity. Pick your pain.

The Mistake:

Most businesses try to go in multiple directions simultaneously.

They go up market AND down market.

They stay broad but also try to niche down.

This creates confusion, dilutes messaging, and stretches teams thin.

Pick one. Execute it. Prove it works. Scale it until you can't scale it anymore (which is typically far bigger than you think).

Then - and only then - consider the next move.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Pick one path. Dominate it.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: When It’s Easy, Go Harder
emails.body:
Words I like: Most entrepreneurs celebrate when business gets easy, but the smartest ones recognize that's exactly when they should accelerate.

Mozi Minute: When It's Easy, Go Harder

Business is like a casino. If you keep playing with the same strategy and never change the odds, the house eventually wins.

The market is the house.

Competition is the house.

Bad luck is the house.

They all have infinite time and resources.

You don't.

But here's what most entrepreneurs miss: when you get a good hand, you don't celebrate and coast. You double down.

Most business owners do the opposite. Revenue is flowing, team is running smooth, customers are happy.

So they ease up. Take longer vacations. Focus on "work-life balance."

Big mistake.

Easy times are when you build the moat that protects you during hard times.

When business gets easy, that's your signal to go harder.

Here's why:

Easy means you have excess capacity. Use it to build systems, hire better people, expand into new markets. Don't waste it on comfort.

Easy means you're ahead of competitors. While they're struggling with the problems you've solved, you can pull further ahead. Gaps close fast in business.

Easy means cash flow is strong. This is when you invest in the future, not when you increase personal expenses. Reinvest while you can afford to fail.

Easy is temporary. Markets shift. Competitors catch up. Economic conditions change. The entrepreneurs who survive understand that easy times fund the hard times.

Think of every easy period as borrowed time.

The casino will eventually get its edge back.

Your job is to change the fundamental odds while you have the advantage.

Real examples:

Amazon used profitable quarters to fund AWS.

Apple used iPhone success to build their services ecosystem.

They didn't coast when things got easy - they accelerated.

The casino analogy holds:

When you're winning, increase your bets strategically.

When you're losing, preserve capital and wait for better odds.

But never coast just because you're comfortable.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Easy is your opportunity, not just your reward,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Content Winner CTA Hack
emails.body:
Words I like: The best way to make ads that work is to use ads that already work.

Mozi Minute: Content Winner CTA Hack

Most businesses waste money testing ads from scratch.

They create new content, new hooks, new everything.

Then wonder why most ads flop.

Here's the strategy that's generated half our winning ads (with almost no extra effort):

Step 1: Create lots of content (almost every business can benefit from more content)

Step 2: Find your organic winners - top 10% engagement

Step 3: Record a direct-to-camera CTA for your offer

Step 4: Add one bridge sentence and paste your CTA onto the winning content

That's it.

Examples:

Your gym post about "3 exercises that burn more calories than running" goes viral.

Add: "If you want to see if this applies to your fitness goals, download our free workout plan."

Your accounting tip about "write-offs most people miss" gets massive engagement.

Add: "If you want to see if this applies to your business, fill out our tax calculator and book a call."

Your marketing insight about "why Facebook ads fail" blows up.

Add: "If you want to see if this applies to your campaigns, grab our free audit checklist."

Why this crushes regular ads:

You already know it's a winner - the organic engagement proves it.

The platform rewards good content with cheaper distribution.

You build brand recognition while driving conversions.

Zero creative risk - you're using proven performers.

The customer experience is perfect.

People just consumed valuable content from you.

Your CTA feels like a natural extension, not an interruption.

Implementation:

1. Go through your last 50 posts.

2. Find the top 5 performers.

3. Record 30-second CTAs that match your main offer.

4. Test these as ads immediately.

This strategy works because you're not hoping content will work - you're amplifying content that already worked.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Amplify winners, don't guess,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: 2025 Media Strategy
emails.body:
Words I like: Your social profiles are the new business cards, your content is the new website, and your media team is the new sales team.

Mozi Minute: 2025 Media Strategy

Most businesses still think of content creation and paid advertising as separate departments.

Wrong.

In 2025, they're the same thing.

If I were starting over today, I'd structure my entire media approach around one integrated team that does both.

Here's why:

Modern credibility:

Your profiles are your new website.

When someone hears about you, they check your social first.

No content = no credibility. It's the modern "we're legit" business card.

Free lead generation:

Quality content attracts prospects without ad spend.

They find you, consume your expertise, then reach out. Inbound beats outbound every time.

Sales tool library:

Create content answering every objection and use case.

When prospects have specific questions, send them the relevant video instead of explaining it again.

Scales your sales conversations.

Proven ad creative:

Your best-performing content becomes your ads. Just add CTAs to winners.

No creative risk, better performance, lower costs.

Team efficiency:

The same people creating content can create ads.

Same skills, same tools, same understanding of what works.

One team, multiple revenue streams.

This is the future of marketing departments.

Not separate content teams and ad teams.

One integrated media team that creates once and distributes everywhere.

This is how I'm doing it.

The old way:

Marketing team buys ads, content team makes posts, sales team handles objections separately.

The new way:

Media team creates content that generates leads, converts prospects, and becomes advertising creative simultaneously.

Implementation:

1. Start with one person who can write, record video.

2. Have them create daily content, track what performs.

3. Then you turn winners into ads.

4. Scale from there.

The businesses that merge content creation with advertising will dominate.

The ones that keep them separate will get left behind.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Create once, distribute everywhere,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: How To Scale Faster
emails.body:
Words I like: By the time you have all the information to make an informed decision, the opportunity will be gone.

Mozi Minute: Speed of Decisions

The speed of your decision making is the speed of your business.

Most entrepreneurs think they need complete information before deciding.

Wrong.

You need enough information to move forward, and most times that's way less than you think.

Here's the reality:

You will always have incomplete information.

The market won't wait for you to gather more data.

Competitors won't pause while you deliberate.

Opportunities don't extend their deadlines because you're "being thorough."

The solution:

Learn to distinguish between one-way doors and two-way doors.

One-way doors are truly irreversible decisions.

Selling your company. Firing a key employee. Shutting down a division.

These deserve careful consideration because you can't easily undo them.

Two-way doors are reversible.

Most business decisions fall here:

Hiring someone. Launching a product. Testing a new price. Or a new offer.

You can walk back through these doors if needed.

Most entrepreneurs (and especially their teams) treat two-way door decisions like one-way door decisions.

They spend weeks analyzing reversible choices.

This kills momentum and hands opportunities to faster competitors.

The speed hack: Make two decisions simultaneously.

Decision 1: Do this.

Decision 2: If this doesn't work, do this instead.

Example:

"We're launching the premium service next month. If we don't get 50 signups in 60 days, we'll pivot to the basic version and test different messaging."

This lets you move immediately while building in your pivot plan. You're not hoping it works - you're planning for both outcomes.

Implementation framework:

Ask: Is this reversible? (Most are).

If yes, decide quickly with Plan A and Plan B.

Set clear success metrics and timelines.

Move fast, measure results, adjust accordingly.

Remember:

It's usually faster to decide immediately then course-correct than to wait for perfect information that never comes.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Decide fast, adjust faster,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Big Ticket Retention = Distribution
emails.body:
Words I like: For big-ticket businesses, revenue retention isn't about selling more boats to the same customer - it's about recruiting dealers who sell your boats every month.

Mozi Minute: Bulky Purchase Revenue Retention

Most businesses selling expensive, infrequent purchases think retention doesn't apply to them.

"How can I retain a customer who buys a house once every 4 years?"

Wrong question.

The right question: "How do I retain the revenue stream without needing the same customer to buy again?"

Here's the breakthrough:

For bulky purchases, retention happens at the seller level, not the customer level.

Think about it:

Car manufacturers don't need you buying cars monthly. They need dealerships selling cars monthly.

Boat builders don't need repeat boat buyers. They need marina dealers moving inventory consistently.

Home builders don't need the same family buying houses repeatedly. They need real estate agents who consistently recommend them.

BIG AHA:

Your retention metric isn't customer lifetime value - it's seller lifetime value.

How to build seller-based retention:

1. Recruit sales partners who move your product regularly (dealers, agents, distributors).

2. Provide them with better margins, training, and support than competitors.

3. Create exclusive territories or preferred pricing tiers

4. Build long-term contracts with performance bonuses.

5. Track revenue per seller, not just revenue per customer

The enterprise value difference is massive:

A boat company dependent on finding individual buyers every month has no predictable revenue stream.

A boat company with 50 dealers who each sell 2 boats monthly has a $10M+ re-ocurring revenue business.

Customer retention still matters for referrals and reputation. But seller retention creates the predictable cash flow that makes your business sellable.

Implementation:

1. List every person/business that could sell your product regularly.

2. Rank them by potential monthly volume.

3. Start recruiting the top 10 with exclusive deals they can't refuse.

This transforms your business from a marketing-dependent venture into a distribution-based asset.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Retain sellers, retain revenue,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Management Diamond
emails.body:
Words I like: Most managers try to fix the person when they should be fixing the process.

Mozi Minute: Management Diamond

When someone doesn't do their job, there are only five possible reasons.

Telling your employees about this framework makes the conversation an easy "problem solving" exercise rather than a blame-shifting one.

Just state the facts:

"Hey I wanted this done by this time and it wasn't. There are five reasons that's possible, I just want to figure out which one it is so we can make sure it doesn't happen again."

1. They don't know WHAT you want.

"You didn't know WHAT I wanted you to do."

You think you communicated clearly but you didn't.

Solution → Communication: Write it down.

We have a saying "If it isn't written, it doesn't exist."

2. They don't know HOW to do it.

"You knew I wanted it done, but I never trained you on how I wanted it done (or how to do it)."

They understand what you want but lack the skills.

Solution → Training: You want them to close deals but never taught them your sales process.

3. They don't know WHEN you want it.

"You knew what I wanted and how to do it, I just didn't tell you by when."

You assigned a task with no deadline then got mad when it wasn't done yesterday.

Solution → Deadlines: Every task needs a "done by" date.

To this day I still use "WHO-WHAT-WHEN" at the end of every meeting to wrap it up.

And nothing makes me happier than seeing my managers wrap every meeting with it to make sure nothing is missed.

It then gets put in a project management tool - and now it exists!

4. They don't WANT to do it.

"You knew what I wanted, how to do it, when I wanted it done by, but you weren't motivated to do it."

Either the incentive isn't strong enough or the consequence for not doing it isn't clear.

Note: This could be a real world consequence. This isn't necessarily a punishment from you.

Most people will avoid getting fired, but the best performers do things because they actually enjoy the work itself.

5. Something's blocking them.

"You knew what I wanted, how to do it, when I wanted it done by, you were motivated to do it, but something stopped you."

Ex: Like asking your chef to make an omelet when there are no eggs in the kitchen.

Here's how to use this in real conversations:

"Hey Sarah, you didn't complete the project. Let's figure out why so we can fix it. Did you know I wanted you to do it? Did you know how to do it? Did you know I needed it by Friday? Were you motivated to do it? Was something blocking you?"

Most employees will immediately tell you which one it is. Then you fix that specific thing instead of having vague conversations about performance.

Real example:

I had an editing team that kept missing deadlines. Turns out the office internet was slower than their home internet.

We paid for fiber optic and the problem disappeared. It wasn't a motivation issue or a skill issue. It was just a blocker we needed to remove.

The next time someone isn't doing what you want…

Before you get mad at them, ask yourself:

- Do they know WHAT to do?
- Do they know HOW to do it?
- Do they know WHEN to do it?
- Do they know WHY they should do it?
- Is something blocking them?

Diagnose first. Then fix the actual problem.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Stay awesome

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Revenue Escape Velocity
emails.body:
Words I like: WOM > Churn = Endless Growth

Mozi Minute: Revenue Escape Velocity

Most subscription businesses fight an uphill battle every month.

They're bleeding customers faster than they're gaining them through referrals and organic growth.

This creates a dependency on paid acquisition that never ends. Smart businesses flip this equation.

Escape velocity = when your % word-of-mouth growth exceeds your % churn rate consistently.

Here's the math that changes everything:

Monthly churn rate: 5%

Monthly word-of-mouth/organic growth rate: 3%

Result: 3% - 5% → You're shrinking by 2% monthly despite marketing efforts

Flip it:

Monthly churn rate: 3%

Monthly word-of-mouth growth rate: 5%

Result: 5% - 3% → You're growing by 2% monthly without any other advertising.

The magic happens when word-of-mouth exceeds churn.

Your business becomes self-sustaining.

Marketing becomes optional instead of essential.

Growth compounds naturally.

How to reach escape velocity:

1. Reduce churn through better onboarding and customer success.

2. Increase referrals by systematically asking satisfied customers.

3. Improve product quality so customers can't help but talk about you.

4. Create shareable moments in your customer experience.

Pro Tip:

Track both percentages monthly. Most businesses only track churn.

The winners obsess over word-of-mouth rate just as much.

Example: If 100 customers refer 1 person each over 12 months, that's 8.3% monthly word-of-mouth growth.

If your churn is 5%, you're growing 3.3% monthly without spending a dollar on ads.

Once you hit escape velocity, paid marketing becomes rocket fuel instead of life support.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Make WOM > churn,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Activation Rebates
emails.body:
Words I like: If they pay, they pay attention.

Mozi Minute: Activation Rebates

Most businesses lose money on customers who never activate.

They buy your service, skip onboarding, ignore your calls, then churn after month two.

You blame the customer.

Wrong.

You didn't incentivize the right behavior.

Here's the fix:

Pay customers to activate themselves.

The math is simple:

An activated customer might be worth $15,000 to your business.

A non-activated customer might be worth $1,000.

That $14,000 difference is your activation value.

Smart businesses spend a fraction of that gap to drive activation behavior.

Real examples that work:

Software company: "$200 credit when you complete onboarding and connect your first integration within 7 days."

Coaching program: "$2000 rebate when you attend your first 4 calls and submit your action plan."

Marketing agency: "$1500 back when you provide login access and approve your first 3 campaigns."

Fitness program: "$150 rebate when you complete week 1 workouts and submit your progress photos."

The psychology:

People hate losing money they've already earned (especially if they bought because of it).

Once they complete the first activation step for a rebate, they're more likely to complete the rest. And put the rebate at the end.

Actions:

- Calculate your activated vs non-activated customer lifetime value

- Identify the specific actions that predict long-term success

- Offer rebates equal to 10-25% of the activation value gap

- Make the timeline tight (7-14 days max)

- Track which rebates drive the highest long-term retention

- Add the rebate at the end of the term or contract

Pro tip:

Frame it as earning money back, not getting a discount upfront.

"Complete onboarding and earn $200 back" hits different than "Get onboarding help for $200 off."

Goal:

Instead of hoping customers do what's best for them, you're paying them to do what's best for both of you.

Activation rates jump. Retention follows. LTV multiplies.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Pay for the behavior you want,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Expensive Leads Fix
emails.body:
Words I like: Your leads rarely cost too much, you make too little from them. But if they really do cost too much, there are 6 fixes.

Mozi Minute: Expensive Leads Fix

If your leads cost more than industry benchmarks…you're profitable, but barely.

Most businesses panic and cut ad spend or chase cheaper platforms.

Wrong move.

Here's the hierarchy of fixes that actually work:

Fix #1: Better Offer (Fastest ROI).

Faster, Do more for them, Remove more risk - or all three.

And when in doubt - deliver faster. Test it for one week.

Even a modest increase in CTRs/lead conversion can drop lead cost significantly. No new traffic needed.

Fix #2: CRO Your Funnel (Medium effort, high return)

Track every step: landing page to call booked to show rate to close rate. Find your biggest leak and plug it first.

Most businesses lose the majority of leads between booking and showing. Fix that before buying more leads.

Fix #3: Better Ad/Content Creative

Look at your last 100 ads or creatives.

Look at the hooks. Reuse the best 10 and make 10 pieces/ads with each of them. Rinse & repeat.

Fix #4: Nurture Your List (Long-term gold)

That lead who didn't buy today might buy in months. Build an email sequence that provides value weekly.

I've seen businesses dramatically increase revenue just by properly following up with existing leads.

Fix #5: Content Marketing if you aren't already (Slower start, compounds forever)

Start creating helpful content in your space. In the short term it'll increase your conversion on outbound and ads.

Long term, it takes time to gain traction, but eventually generates more free leads than your other avenues.

Fix #6: Referral System (Underused goldmine)

Your best customers know other people like them. Create a simple referral program. Track it.

Referrals typically close much higher than cold leads and cost significantly less.

Fix #7: Review Optimization (Free but takes work)

More reviews = higher conversion rates from the same traffic.

Get aggressive about collecting reviews. Respond to all of them. Businesses with many reviews convert much better than those with few.

The hierarchy matters.

Start with offer improvements and funnel fixes.

Those give you immediate relief.

Then build the long-term engines.

Most businesses skip straight to "find cheaper traffic" and wonder why they're stuck in a race to the bottom.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Make leads worth more, don't pay less,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Proof First Business
emails.body:
Words I like: Most businesses ask for reviews after the work is done, but smart businesses build proof creation into the work itself.

Mozi Minute: Proof First Business

Most businesses treat testimonials like an afterthought.

They finish the work, then awkwardly ask "Hey, would you mind leaving us a review?"

By then, the magic moment has passed.

The customer is already thinking about their next problem.

You missed your shot.

Here's the fix: Build proof collection into your service delivery. Don't ask for it later. Capture it during.

Real examples that work:

Gym: Take before/after photos on day 1 and day 30. "This is for your progress tracking." Then ask permission to use the best transformations for marketing.

Consultant: Record a quick video recap after each breakthrough session. "This is for your records." Half become testimonials when you ask later.

Web designer: Screenshot the old site before you start work. Send side-by-side comparisons during the project. "Look at this progress!" Most clients volunteer to share these.

Accountant: Document the tax savings in a simple one-page summary. "Here's what we saved you this year." Those numbers become case studies.

The psychology:

When you're actively documenting their wins, customers feel more value. When they see visual proof of progress, they want to share it.

Pro tip:

Always ask permission to document first.

"Would it be okay if we track your progress visually? It helps us deliver better results."

They'll say yes, and you've pre-sold them on the idea of sharing later.

What to do next:

- Capture "before" on every customer

- Identify your "proof moments" - when customers see clear value

- Build documentation into those moments naturally

- Make the documentation benefit them first (progress tracking, records, etc.)

- Ask permission upfront to capture these moments

- Follow up later with "Can we share your success story?"

This turns every customer into potential marketing material without being pushy.

And you only need to put work into the ones who work out spectacularly.

For everyone else, it's just ammo for upsells later.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Proof >

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Talent Recruiting Ladder
emails.body:
Words I like: The method that finds your best people at one level will fail miserably at the next level up.

Mozi Minute: Talent Recruitment Ladder

Most businesses use the same recruiting approach for every role.

They post on Indeed, hope for the best, then wonder why they can't find quality talent.

Wrong strategy.

Different levels of talent hang out in different places and respond to different approaches.

Here's my 4-tier recruitment ladder:

Level 1: Low to Mid-Level Jobs

Use traditional job platforms - Indeed, LinkedIn Jobs, Glassdoor.

These people are actively job hunting and respond to standard postings. Cast a wide net, filter fast.

Level 2: Mid-Level or Specialized Roles

Hit job-specific forums and niche groups. Facebook groups for marketing managers, GitHub for developers, industry Slack communities.

These people aren't desperately job hunting but will move for the right opportunity.

Level 3: Leaders

Direct outreach. They're not browsing job boards. Find them on LinkedIn, Twitter, or industry events. Message them personally.

They need to be recruited, not attracted. "You look perfect for a role we're hiring for. Do you know anyone else like you who might be interested."

They'll still respond but it seems less aggressive.

Level 4: High-Level Executives

Blue chip headhunters exclusively. C-suite executives have relationships with specific search firms.

They take calls from Korn Ferry, not from your HR department.

The mistake:

Using Level 1 tactics to find Level 4 people, or vice versa.

A $300k VP won't respond to your Indeed posting. A $50k coordinator won't get recruited by executive search firms.

Match your method to the level. Save time. Get better results.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: How To Reinvest Profits
emails.body:
Words I like: The businesses that reinvest their profits grow exponentially. The ones that don't grow linearly until they plateau.

Mozi Minute: How To Reinvest Profits

Most service businesses hit a wall because they don't know where to reinvest their profits.

On the other hand, manufacturing companies have obvious reinvestment - better equipment, more capacity, R&D.

But when you're selling expertise and time, it's less clear (which today still represents nearly 80% of businesses).

So owners pocket the profit and wonder why growth stalls.

Here's the truth:

You always want to reinvest in whatever gets you the highest return.

For most businesses, that's the business itself. But service businesses have two specific places that extra profit should go.

Marketing & Brand:

This gives you pricing power and retention. When you're known in your market, you can charge 2-3x more than competitors.

Plus, strong brands have lower churn rates.

That future pricing power and retention turn into future profits that compound.

Talent:

This gives you capacity and optionality. Better people can handle bigger clients, start new divisions, or make your company sellable without you.

Each A-player hire multiplies your ability to serve more clients at higher price points.

The math is simple:

A $2M service business that reinvests 30% of profits into marketing and talent for 3 years will outperform the one that pockets the profit every single time.

One builds enterprise value.

The other builds a higher-paying job.

Example: Agency owner making $500k profit.

Option 1: Take it all home.

Option 2: Invest $200k in brand building and $150k in senior talent.

Year three, the first owner still makes $500k.

The second owner's business is worth $5M and throws off $1.2M annually.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Reinvest to multiply, don't extract to survive,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: How to Setup Profitshares
emails.body:
Words I like: The only thing that's guaranteed about your business in 10 years is that you'll still be here. Everything else is conjecture.

Mozi Minute: Profitshares

Many employees want to participate in the upside. Few actually have the skills necessary to merit it.

They typically ask for equity without knowing the implications (taxes etc).

So I have a 4-part explanation that helps explain all the elements of equity and helps create something that accomplishes the same objective but protects them and you.

Profitshare = % of cash flow of the business as it operates

Sale bonus = % of money gotten from a sale in the future

Risk = they probably don't want this, but it comes with equity

Control = which you're probably not gonna give

Enter "phantom equity" or "synthetic equity". It goes by different names but the idea is the same.

Instead of agreeing on a valuation, and giving them permanent equity, which then they have to pay taxes on, which is often more than most can afford if appropriately valued…you just say:

"How about I give you what you want which is some share of the profits, and in the event we sell some portion of the proceeds."

Most people are down for this. Then you just need to talk about percentages and timelines.

Note: You can have a delay before these features turn on. This is called a vesting cliff.

Meaning, they don't get to participate for a year etc. then it turns on. And you can have it vest in waves. Like they get 1% per year for 4 years.

But here's the important part:

If they decide the company isn't for them anymore, or you don't think they're for the company anymore...

That synthetic equity comes back to you to give to the next person who is coming in to help you grow. And you don't keep bleeding portions of your business over time.

This way is fast and easy and doesn't trigger a tax event.

Note: just because you know how to do this doesn't mean you SHOULD. It's still a very valuable thing. After all, there are only 100 percentage points.

So if you even think it's worth doing, be smart about how much and to whom to give it to.

As promised, value in under a minute (ish).

You're not overexpanding, you're under-talenting,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: SPCL Influence
emails.body:
Words I like: The whole point of marketing is to become trusted. Once you're trusted, you don't need anything else.

Mozi Minute: SPCL Influence

Many business owners make random content hoping to gain influence. But they don't actually know what they're doing. And that's fine.

But, if you'd like to get more out for what you put in (leverage), here's how I think about it.

Influence = the likelihood someone follows your directions

The elements that increase influence = SPCL

Status = you control things the prospect finds valuable. Ex = you have money and you can demonstrate it.

Power = they followed your directions in the past and had a good thing happen. Ex = you say to add a 2-step optin to increase optin rates. They do it. Then it does. So the next time you give a directive they are more likely to follow it.

Credibility = you have proof. Ex = you've won awards, or have visual proof, around whatever thing you're telling them to do.

Likeness = do you look and act like them. This doesn't have to be physical, but physically looking similar will increase influence. This is why advertising campaigns benefit from multiple different avatars.

Note: some of these can be accomplished at the same time.

Ex: me selling a company provides both status and credibility. I sold the company which gives me credibility. I got lots of money for it which gives status.

Alternatively, I could just have money which would not give me credibility for selling a company (ex = trust fund kid inherits money).

Or, I could have sold a company but got paid almost nothing because I had diluted my shares which would not give me status (ex = founder who takes on tons of debt and leaves empty handed but knows how to scale).

So next time you make content...

Try to show you have what they want, give them directions on how to get it, show proof of 3rd party or observable things you could only achieve with following the advice you're giving, be yourself (and you'll attract people like you).

As promised, value in under a minute.

Build a reputation then protect it with your life,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: PREsuasion Checkbox
emails.body:
Words I like: Words persuade better than features, and commitment persuades better than words.

Mozi Minute: Pre-suasion Checkbox Hack

Most opt-in pages try to sell people on signing up. Wrong approach.

You should sell them on what comes after signing up.

We just tested this with a simple checkbox addition and got a 16% revenue per visitor boost during our launch.

Here's what we did:

Added a checkbox above the opt-in that said "I want a VIP ticket."

That's it.

80% of people checked the box. And here's the best part - the checkbox did absolutely nothing functional - it was pure psychology.

But it worked.

Why this crushed the control:

People who checked the box had already mentally committed to wanting the upsell before they even became leads.

When they saw the actual upsell (upgrading their order to VIP), they were more likely to take it.

The psychology:

Robert Cialdini calls this "pre-suasion" - the moment before persuasion when you prime someone's mental state.

We weren't asking them to buy.

We were asking them to admit they wanted to see what we'd show them.

Implementation is dead simple:

- Add a checkbox above your opt-in form

- Make it about wanting to see the upsell on the next page (whatever that next step is)

- Don't gate anything behind it

- Track the difference in conversion rates

Pure words. Zero tech changes. 16% more revenue from the same traffic.

Considering we did $7M in presales…that's roughly ~$1M in extra earnings from this one test.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Small hinges open big doors,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Hate Translation
emails.body:
Words I like:

Hatred is just someone announcing that you don't match their preferences. Once you see it that way, it loses its teeth.

Mozi Minute: Hate Translation

You will get hate from customers, past employees, family, acquaintances, and strangers on the internet. That's a fact.

Not knowing how to deal with it can spiral you out of doing what you need to, to get where you wanna go.

Here's my best tactic for dealing with hate:

Translate everything into "He lives his life according to his own preferences rather than mine." That's all hate really is. Someone stating you don't match their preferences.

Watch how it works:

- "You work too much" = "I prefer people who work less"

- "You're too aggressive" = "I prefer people who are passive"

- "You charge too much" = "I prefer people who are cheaper"

Once you see this pattern, hate loses its power.

Because here's the truth: Some people will hate you for doing stuff. Others will hate you for not doing stuff.

Some will hate you for being yourself. Others will hate you for not being yourself enough.

The math is simple: Hatred is a fixed cost of success.

You're going to get it either way. So you might as well do you and be yourself. At least then the hate is honest.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Hate is just preferences,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Embrace Your Weirdness
emails.body:
Words I like:

You can't be normal and expect abnormal results.

Normal inputs = normal outputs.

Abnormal inputs = abnormal outputs. This email is inspired by Alex Becker.

Mozi Minute: Embrace Your Weirdness

Here's the math that'll change your perspective:

- Only 1 in 1,000 Americans make over $1M per year.

- 1 in 11,000 make over $10M.

- 1 in 35,000 make over $20M.

If you want those numbers, you must accept this truth: You're trying to be a statistical outlier.

Picture this:

To make $1M/yr, you need to outwork your entire high school graduating class.

To make $10M/yr, you need to be the best person from every high school in your city combined.

To make $20M+/yr, imagine a football stadium with 35,000 people - you have to outlast every single one of them.

That means 999 out of 1,000 people won't understand your choices. Your work hours. Your sacrifices. Your obsessions.

And that's exactly how it has to be.

The moment you start apologizing for working weekends, skipping social events, or saying no to "normal" activities - you're negotiating.

You're not supposed to be relatable. You're supposed to be the 1 person willing to do what the other 99, 999, or 34,999 won't.

And that's why you get what the 999 don't.

Just a friendly reminder to be who you say you are and do what you came here to do.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Be the outlier,

Alex

PS - People who advocate for work life balance are usually mediocre at both. You can also be unbalanced for a season to be more balanced over a lifetime. Expand your timeline.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Ham & The Garlic
emails.body:
Words I like: You have to give people what they want before you can give them what they need.

Mozi Minute: The Ham & The Garlic

A boy begged his father for a dog.

Father said yes, but only if the boy promised to take care of it. Deal.

One day, the dog got ticks while dad was at work.

The boy, panicked, ran to grandma.

"Give the dog garlic," she said. "It'll kill the ticks."

The boy tried. The dog refused. He came back crying. "Grandma, it won't work!"

She smiled. "You don't just give the dog garlic. You wrap it in ham first."

The boy wrapped the garlic in ham. The dog ate it happily. Ticks disappeared.

Day saved.

Moral: Your marketing is the ham. Your product is the garlic.

People don't want "healthy lifestyle" - that's garlic.

They want "lose 10 pounds in 30 days" - that's ham.

Once they trust you can deliver the quick win, they'll believe you can help with the long-term change.

Don't lead with what they need. Lead with what they want. Then, once you've earned their trust, give them what they actually need.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Ham first, garlic second,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: One Thing
emails.body:
Words I like: If you have a list of priorities, you only prove you have none.

Mozi Minute: One Thing, All In

How to be productive: Don't do anything besides the task you set out to do. That's literally it.

Your business probably has too many priorities. I can say that because we all have "priorities" plural.

But it makes no sense. Priority comes from the word priori - which means first or coming before. Which means - there can only be one.

But it's really hard to pick just one. But you have to. After all, if you can't make the decision - how do you expect your team to?

Ex: In late 2019, I decided to build a brand. I figured, if I do nothing else but this, everything else will get accomplished.

That meant that I rerouted almost all my personal attention and resources towards making this happen.

And it paid off.

But it was hard because there were a lot of other things I wanted to do.

So here's the process: Look at your list of priorities. Cross out everything except the biggest one.

The one that - if accomplished - makes everything else irrelevant. That means you take your list of five, and narrow it down to one. And there's always one.

If you can't pick, you're not prioritizing - you're waiting for circumstances to do the deciding for you.

Real prioritization forces tradeoffs. It hurts. Good. That pain means you're doing it right.

Ask yourself: "If all we did was this one thing, and it 2x-3x-5x'd our business, why would we care about doing anything else?" - that's your priority.

Everything else is distraction dressed up as strategy.

The fastest growing companies obsess over one metric. And they think really hard about that metric.

Pick one thing. Go all in. Forget the rest and watch the seas part as everyone knows exactly where you're going and why.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Pick one, win big, Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: LTV:CAC Reality Check
emails.body:
Words I like: Your ability to tolerate boredom while repeating the same action determines your odds of success.

Mozi Minute: LTV:CAC Reality Check

Most founders chase the magic 3:1 LTV:CAC ratio like it's a rule. I've found that's wrong to do for most real businesses.

That 3:1 standard only works if you've built an automated machine across...

- Getting leads (Paid Ads VS Manual Outreach),

- Converting leads (checkout page VS 1-1 salesmen),

- and delivering to them (software/physical products VS service).

And fun fact - and most of us haven't. The less automated each step, the higher that ratio needs to be.

Here's the real ratio you need to scale based on what I've found:

- If all three parts run themselves (getting leads + selling + fulfillment): 3:1 works

- Two parts run themselves: you need 6:1 at least

- One part runs itself: aim for 9:1

- Nothing runs itself (all steps involve people): you need at least 12:1+

→ And remember, these are minimums, not goals.

I like to be much higher than this.

Why? Because when you grow, things break:

- New hires don't work as well as your best people

- You have to find leads in harder spots

- Your systems start to strain

- Infrastructure costs money
- Leads take longer to buy
- Work gets harder to do right

Bottom line: the more people have to do the work in your business, the more money you need to make on each sale to grow safely.

This is my rule of thumb. Score your business and see how you do.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Save the cheerleader, save the world.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Biggest Risk in Business
emails.body:
Words I like: The greatest risk to any business is that the founder doesn't want to do it anymore. You just gotta want it more than you hate what it takes to get it.

Mozi Minute: Founder Passion

No one talks about the biggest risk to your company: you falling out of love with it.

When a founder loses their love, the business is as good as dead.

So here's my most important rule: don't hate your business. Prioritize removing anything or anyone you hate, then worry about the rest.

Some Real Examples I Heard Within The Last 30 Days:

If there's a team member who drains your soul - fire them.

I don't care how "good" they seem. No one is worth you hating Monday mornings.

That product that's making good money but making you sad? Cut it.

That office that's "smart" but feels awful? Move.

That website you hate looking at? Change it now.

I know this sounds illogical and unlike my normal emails. But I met with probably 4 different businesses over the last two weeks - big businesses - whose only risk was the founder hating their lives.

Building a business is a long run, not a quick dash.

The middle part - years #3 through #7 - that's where most founders quit.

Not because the business failed, but because they couldn't stand it anymore.

Remember: the road is long and the load is heavy.

First rule: set up everything so you don't give up. Then you can be smart about the rest. Because nothing works if you stop pushing.

If you want to overpay someone because they make your life easier - do it.

If you want a big fancy office because its been your dream - do it.

If you want to get rid of someone who is otherwise competent because you just cant stand them - do it.

It's your business. You make the rules. So don't make rules you hate. You only have you, to blame.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Love it or cut it,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Rush is Imaginary
emails.body:
Words I like: The faster you chase success, the slower it seems to arrive. The slow way is the fast way because the fast way never gets you there.

Mozi Minute: Rush is Imaginary

Most founders think pushing harder and moving faster is the key to growth.

I've seen the opposite.

The businesses most obsessed with "growth hacking" and "scaling fast" often grow the slowest - because they get stuck fighting fires (for years).

It looks like a plateau but feels like drowning.

Here's why:

Rushing creates short-term thinking, which creates long-term problems. Think about it.

When you rush:

- You hire fast (and don't train or onboard). You skip systems to "move quick."
- You patch problems instead of fixing them. You make snap choices you later regret.
- You launch a quick new service or product without adding infrastructure - you "double time" a team that's already spread thin.

It doesn't work. Believe me, I've tried.

The math is simple:

Every "shortcut" you take today creates two new problems tomorrow.

Those problems each create two more.

Soon you're drowning in issues that all trace back to one thing "a rush" - an arbitrary goal that you set for yourself that you said if we do not hit this I will be unhappy.

And in trying to pursue it, you guarantee your failure.

Let me bust a myth:

Unless you're in a true winner-take-all market with network effects (hint: you're probably not), the race isn't about speed. It's about lasting.

Most markets have room for many winners.

Your real job isn't to be first - it's to still be standing when others fall.

And when you stay in business for a long time - and focus on one thing - you tend to get pretty good at it.

And when you get pretty good at stuff and do it for a long time - you tend to build a big business.

And that's the point.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Sometimes you gotta go slow to go fast,

Alex
