emails.subject: Launch Bonus Expires In ~60min…
emails.body:
I just revealed the greatest offer I've ever made.

Here's what you get when you get 200+ copies of Money Models:

- ACQ Implementation Virtual Workshop led entirely by me based on the exact workshop we do in-person for $35,000 - I will be answering your questions while taking you through my…

- Proprietary ACQ Deconstraining Process where I guide you step-by-step through our entire deconstraining process to help laser-focus you on THE constraint on your 10x growth.

- Supply & Demand Amplification to reveal whether your bottleneck is demand generation or supply optimization.

- Find & Create Leverage by identifying exact constraints to get maximum returns on your effort… and my battle tested frameworks to help you realize it.

- Crystal Clear Personalized Implementation Plan showing exactly which constraint you annihilate first and my best tactics for doing it to help you leverage the most 10x growth you can.

- 12 ACQ Playbooks - My most detailed deep dive into all the profit-boosting business tactics we use at ACQ. These are PHYSICAL copies only.

- Closing Handbook - 64 pages on closing more deals
- Proof Checklist - 32 pages on better persuasion
- Retention Playbook - 42 pages on keeping customers longer
- Marketing Machine - 32 pages on ads without your face
- Lifetime Value - 32 pages on getting customers to spend more
- Hooks Playbook - 30 pages on turning eyeballs into interest
- Branding Playbook - 42 pages on truly influential branding
- Price Raise Playbook - 32 pages on getting customers to spend more money on the same stuff
- Promo Cash Playbook - 36 pages of ready to use promotions that work in every industry I've tried
- GOATed Ads - 36 pages of frameworks to make and scale the thousands of ads for this launch and everything else we sell
- Lead Nurture - 46 pages on actually getting customers to show

- My Custom Trained ACQ AI integrating over $28,000,000 of real-world consulting lessons, all my books, all twelve advanced playbooks, and my never-before-released writings to train a custom AI that…

- Finally Answers "What Would Alex Do In My Business?" Trained on my years of consultations, stats, breakthroughs, and results to show precise actions I would take in your business.

- Puts $28M of Real-World Consulting At Your Fingertips with realtime access to all identified constraints, designed solutions, and verified implementation steps from1000's of internal ACQ reports.

- Your $135,000 Answer Fine-Tuned An Instant based on the exact tactics over 226+ businesses paid $135,000 each to get…faster than it takes to make your coffee.

- Engineers Hyper-Relevant Solutions from having engineered solutions for 226+ unique businesses—from B2B Services to B2C Services to SaaS to Physical Products— chances are it will engineer a solution for business just like yours.

- Like Having My ACQ Killers On-Call for you 24/7 to attack your exact problem day or night. This AI has more real business wisdom than people get in their entire careers.

- Grants Access To The "De-Classified" ACQ Data Vault. This is the first time I've made anything like the business intelligence, data, and validated solutions at this level available to anyone. At any point. Ever.

Also Free: My Entire Launch Blackbook (200+ pages) with all the emails, texts, ads, video sales letters, the presentation itself, the agenda…everything we used to promote this launch that you can use to launch anything you want.

This goes away the MOMENT the live stream ends.

If you're on the fence, do it. This is two years of work, and thirteen years of trial and error consolidated into checklists you can immediately use in your business.

All these are free when you buy 200 copies of Money Models

You won't regret it,

Alex

PS - For big business owners: who want to work with me for free for six months Look for the one-time offer for 800 copies after you buy 200 copies

PPS - If you're worried about distributing the books, don't worry. I've got your back.

You'll get a code to give out 199 free copies to friends/employees/audience. They can claim theirs anytime over the next year. We'll ship it right to them (they just cover shipping & handling).

Books not redeemed by August 19, 2026, will be automatically donated to entrepreneurs who need them.

emails.subject: SHH it's a SURPRISE!!
emails.body:
Hey it's Leila!

It's Alex's birthday weekend!

I'd love to surprise him with a flood of social media posts wishing him a happy birthday + good luck for the 100M Money Models live event tomorrow (as a surprise).

SURPRISE means it's a secret - Alex has no idea!!

Here's an idea of what I was hoping to do…

For anyone who would like to get involved please:

- Post a quick selfie or video tagging him @hormozi
- Wish Alex a happy birthday
- And wish him good luck!

Or let him know you'll see him virtually tomorrow when he releases his brand-new book live!

I'd love to see your posts (fully encourage y'all to be creative).

So many of you have unique ideas and personalities, I'm making a little compilation for him & I think y'all will make it way better than I ever could.

(And if you would rather not, feel free to ignore this message.)

TL;DR:

To participate, please post something on social media, tag Alex @hormozi, and wish him a happy birthday for me.

I'd love to make this a birthday Alex won't forget.

Thank you all!

Looking forward to seeing you all tomorrow at the virtual event too.

- Leila

PS - For those who skipped the wall of text above lol

Alex's birthday is tomorrow! I'd love it if everyone could help surprise him & show some support for the book launch tomorrow.

Send him a quick Happy Birthday post @hormozi and wish him good luck.

And if you are showing up, let him know you'll be there to support him.

Thank you.

PPS - feel free to dress up like Alex (one feature makes the most sense, like hair, beard or outfit).

This means the book demonstrates tactics that actually work.

This is the most powerful book I've ever written - if we define power as the aggregate change in business owners lives.

Expect fireworks.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Close Rate Pricing Guide
emails.body:
Words I like:

Dear younger self→The less a customer pays, the more problems they cause.

Mozi Minute: Close Rate Pricing Guide

I see businesses everywhere obsess over closing more deals when they should be obsessing over charging more money.

Your close rate isn't just a sales metric - it's a pricing diagnostic. And most businesses are leaving cash on the table by ignoring it.

This is my close rate pricing guide cheat sheet (for services):

80%+ close rate → You're underpriced by 3-4x (triple or quadruple your prices)

60-80% close rate → You're underpriced by 2-3x (double or triple your prices)

50-60% close rate → You're underpriced by 1.5-2x (increase prices 50-100%)

40-50% close rate → You're underpriced by 1.25 to 1.5x (increase prices 25-50%)

30-40% close rate → You're probably appropriately priced (maintain)

Below 30% → Focus on selling better before touching price

The math is simple:

If you're closing 8 out of 10 prospects, you're not charging enough. Period. Don't believe me? Test it. Double your price with the next 10 prospects and see what happens.

I've seen this play out with too many companies to count. That being said, if you are in a commoditized market - your prices will be more sensitive. So the key isn't to make the tiny change - the key is to figure out how to not be a commodity - then make a massive change.

Reminder:

The correct price is one in which you hear "no" more than "yes", but you make more money.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Want to find your optimal price point without sacrificing revenue?

We just opened spots for our next Scaling Workshop in Vegas.

We turn away 65% of applicants to keep the room quality high.

Raise prices, raise profits, Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Swamp
emails.body:
Words I like: The hardest part about scaling isn't finding opportunities - it's affording the people who can execute them.

Mozi Minute: The 1M to 3M Swamp

There's a revenue dead zone we call "the swamp" - it's that 1M - 3M range where you're stuck between two terrible choices.

Here's why:

You need higher-level talent to break through, but you can't afford higher-level talent yet.

Picture this:

You run a $2M business with 20% margins.

That's 400K profit.

The VP of Sales you need to hit $10M costs 250K-300K.

Your options?

Risk 75% of your profit on one hire who might not work out, or work double-time doing your current job plus the next-level job that needs doing.

Both suck.

- Option 1: Bet the farm on expensive talent
- Option 2: Work yourself into the ground
- Option 3: Stay comfortable at 400K/year forever

Most pick Option 3.

That's why the swamp is full of $2M businesses that never grow.

The only way out is picking one of the terrible short-term choices.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Swamp
emails.body:
Words I like: The hardest part about scaling isn't finding opportunities - it's affording the people who can execute them.

Mozi Minute: The 1M to 3M Swamp

There's a revenue dead zone we call "the swamp" - it's that 1M - 3M range where you're stuck between two terrible choices.

Here's why:

You need higher-level talent to break through, but you can't afford higher-level talent yet.

Picture this:

You run a $2M business with 20% margins.

That's 400K profit.

The VP of Sales you need to hit $10M costs 250K-300K.

Your options?

Risk 75% of your profit on one hire who might not work out, or work double-time doing your current job plus the next-level job that needs doing.

Both suck.

- Option 1: Bet the farm on expensive talent
- Option 2: Work yourself into the ground
- Option 3: Stay comfortable at 400K/year forever

Most pick Option 3.

That's why the swamp is full of $2M businesses that never grow.

emails.subject: What’s a money model?
emails.body:
What's a Money Model?

A Money Model is a series of offers deliberately structured to generate the most cash up front, get the most leads, and get your highest customer lifetime value. Period.

In simpler terms it attracts more customers, gets them to spend more money, faster, over and over again.

It capitalizes on the scientific principles of volume, intensity, latency, frequency–but weaponised for growing a business.

A proper Money Models includes four parts:

- Attraction Offers: to get the most leads

- Upsell Offers: to get more money from them faster

- Downsell Offers: to turn a "no" into a "yes"  (even more sales)

- Continuity Offers: to keep them buying again and again

It took me years to realize this - but every super successful business I've owned had all four parts perfectly sequenced. The ones that didn't, weren't super successful, and they were like most businesses…

Most businesses putter along with only 1 or 2 parts - but - the magic happens when you combine all the parts together.

With a properly designed Money Model…your business attracts the maximum amount of customers, maximizes profit and cash flow so you can outspend the competition, and builds stability in volatile markets with recurring revenue.

And the best part, the Money Models in my new book work for any business, in any industry, of any size.

I've been waiting for 6 years to unleash the insane power of Money Models - and the time has finally come.

$100M Money Models is coming out on August 16th, 2025.

So if you enjoyed $100M Offers, $100M Leads... or any of my free content, courses, or books I've put out over the last few years, this is definitely for you:

- Register here free

1) Register 2) Show up live. That's it.

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: VSL Sandwich Close
emails.body:
Words I like:

"Work smart not hard" only works when you're competing against dumb competitors.

Mozi Minute: VSL Sandwich Close (Video Sales Letter)

Most sales teams waste half their calls regurgitating the same pitch over and over.

The rep gets bored, sounds rehearsed, and the prospect zones out.

Here's the fix: sandwich every sales conversation around a video sales letter (VSL).

Here's how it works:

- Step 1: Short intro call (5 min)

Build rapport, identify needs, set expectations

- Step 2: Send tailored VSL

Your absolute best pitch with visuals, testimonials, and proof

- Step 3: Decision call

Address specific questions and close

Why this crushes traditional sales: Your pitch is always perfect - not dependent on rep energy or how many calls they've done that day.

Your best proof points are visual - testimonials, before/afters, data visualizations.

Prospects can watch on their time (80% will watch within 24 hours).

Your follow-up call is 100% personalized to their unique situation.

Pro tip: Create a library of 3-5 core VSLs for different customer types or pain points. Your team picks the right one based on the intro call.

And yes, if you have multiple calls you'll need even more VSLs.

Welcome to working hard and smart.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Less pitching, more closing,

Alex

PS - Me right now prepping for this book launch (just for laughs)

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: More Value, Less Stuff
emails.body:
Words I like: Your most profitable features are often the ones your customers forgot they needed, not the ones they demand the loudest.

Mozi Minute: The Silent Feature Pruning

Most businesses keep unprofitable features forever out of fear.

They hold meetings about "how to announce changes" while losing money daily.

Here's the reality: The Standish Group's CHAOS Report found that 64% of software features are rarely or never used.

And a Nielsen Norman Group study showed that complex interfaces with too many features can decrease conversion rates by 20-40%.

Meaning having too much stuff can hurt sales, retention, and profitability. Big problem.

Here's your silent pruning playbook:

- Identify features with low usage OR high support costs
- Remove them without announcement
- Monitor complaints for 14 days
- Analyze who complains (revenue value, customer tenure, LTV)
- Only reverse if valuable customers are affected

Example:

Let's say you have a SaaS company and you removed your "custom export" feature.

Only 3% of users had ever used it, but it generated 22% of support tickets.

After silent removal, just 6 customers complained out of 3,400 users.

When analyzed, you might find those 6 were all on legacy discounted plans and represented less than 1% of revenue.

So you keep the change and see onboarding completion rates jump 17% - new users were getting confused by the complex export options!

Another example:

A membership site removing three "bonus resources" from their offering. Nobody complained.

When they surveyed users months later, nobody even remembered those resources had existed.

But new member conversion improved 8% with the simpler offering.

Bottom line:

Every feature has hidden costs - in development, support, and cognitive load on your customers.

Make sure it's worth it.

In a lot of ways, less stuff really can mean more value.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Keep crushing,

Alex

PS - How it feels to miss my upcoming book launch. (just for laughs)

PPS - If you have dark humor memes or business memes... send em in, I'll cite you as the source.

Hey it's Alex.

100M Money Models - my new book - is coming out on Aug 16th, 2025. Reserve your spot for the live mega book launch event here.

I started writing this when I was preparing for my first big exit (in 2020).

I wanted to crystallize all my highest ROI "money models" in one place.

Once I had them all in one place, I ended up using them all the time. It became my most used asset for growing companies.

I reference it so regularly because a money model is a series of offers deliberately structured to generate the most revenue up front, get the most leads, and get the highest lifetime value.

Simply put - it gets more customers, to spend more, faster, again and again.

They are offer sequences - that when combined are so profitable - they are responsible for the vast majority of my material success.

Nothing else comes close.

That's when I realized all this could actually be organized into a single coherent, and incredibly effective 'cookbook' anyone could follow.

This book is my crowning achievement.

Of all the content I've made, this book will teach you the most.

I've been slaving away on this book behind the scenes for over 4 years.

If you are an aspiring entrepreneur and business owner, this book shows how to win.

It's finally here. It's about to get real.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The 9-Word Cash Email
emails.body:
Words I like: Most businesses write paragraphs when nine words would do.

Mozi Minute: The 9-Word Cash Email

Most businesses send long-winded re-engagement emails that get ignored. But there's a simple 9-word format that cuts through the noise and generates instant cash from cold lists. The marketer Dean Jackson pioneered this, and it works across every industry I've tested.

Here's the exact template:

"Are you still looking to [achieve specific goal]?"

That's it. No fancy intro. No long explanation. No pitch.

Real examples that work:

Gym: "Are you still looking to lose those last 20 pounds?"

Real Estate: "Are you still interested in selling your house?"

B2B: "Are you still trying to hire senior developers?"

Coaching: "Are you still wanting to start your own business?"

Marketing: "Are you still looking to get more leads?"

Why it works:

- Creates curiosity (they wonder why you're asking)

- Feels personal (like you remember their goal)

- Easy to respond (just yes/no)

- Zero sales pressure (builds trust)

Who & When to send: Send it to people who haven't bought or engaged in 90+ days. These are leads you've already paid for but written off.

Pro tip: Send on Tuesday morning around 10am. No links. Don't add anything else. Plain text. No formatting. Let them reply naturally. And for God's sake, when this works (and it will), work your darn leads!

As promised, money in under a minute.

Nine words. Fast responses. 0 cost.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Own Your Name
emails.body:
Words I like: If someone's googling your business name and you're not the ad, you're letting competitors bid on your customers.

Mozi Minute: Own Your Name

Here's the easiest money you'll ever make: Buy Google ads for your own business name + every permutation of it.

- "[Your Business]"
- "[Your Business] reviews"
- "[Your Business] pricing"
- "[Your Business] competitors"

Takes 60 minutes to set up. Costs almost nothing.

I have yet to see someone lose on this.

Most businesses don't do this basic move.

Meanwhile, when you control your branded search terms, you get higher click-through rates and lower costs than generic keywords.

The ROAS is typically massive because people searching your name are already warm prospects.

But here's the real kicker:

Don't send that traffic to your website.

Send it to a landing page with an offer/lead magnet.

Someone googling your name is trying to evaluate you. Give them something to evaluate.

Turn browsers into leads instead of letting them bounce around your website and leave - or worse - going to your competitor.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Own your search, Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: My Email Template [Use it]
emails.body:
Words I like: You don't need to be smart. You need to be consistent.

Mozi Money Minute: Email Template

Most business owners struggle with email marketing.

They make it too hard.

They use big words.

They write long blocks of text.

But after testing lots of emails, this format works best for me. And yes, you can copy it.

Why this format works:

- It's easy to scan - Short chunks keep busy people reading

- It sticks to one idea - One tip per email stops overwhelm

- It gives before it asks - Value first, pitch later

- It makes people curious - Subject line + quote hook them

- It ends with a joke - PS with a meme keeps them opening next time

People want both: what they know and what's new.

This format gives them both - a pattern they know with fresh tips inside.

Your open rates will climb the first month using this format. It works.

Copy these parts:

- Your version of "Mozi Money Minute:" in your subject line so they know it's you
- Start with a smart tested quote that sets the mood
- Teach one simple tip they can use today (concept + example)
- Make a one line CTA for your stuff
- Sign off fast
- Add a PS with humor about your topic

As promised, value in under a minute.

Copy, paste, cash in,

Alex

emails.subject: How to do annual renewal fees
emails.body:
Words I like: "If you grow your business without fixing your problems, you grow your problems instead."

Mozi Minute: Annual Renewal Fees

If you have a membership or recurring revenue model, and keep people over a year, a great profit tactic is to add in an annual renewal fee.

It's a one time annual fee on top of the monthly.

It doesn't interfere with front end sales because it's a year away.

And if someone stays a year, they're unlikely to leave.

The fee is usually the price of 1-3 months.

By adding this in, you add 8%-24% to your revenue without incurring any costs.

In a business with 20% margins this could add 50% to your profit.

This also allows you to keep your advertised (and sold) monthly rate lower, while still having a higher blended rate.

For example...

If I charge $39/mo, I might make my annual renewal $99.

This takes my annual revenue per customer from $468 to $567 (a 21% increase).

This makes my effective monthly rate $47/mo rather than $39/mo.

So I get the sales (and churn) of $39 price point but the revenue and profit of a $47 price point.

Effective.

Use it well.

See you soon,

Alex Hormozi

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Membership Offer Stack
emails.body:
Words I like: focus + work ethic + time = wealth

Mozi Minute: Membership Stacks

Most businesses create weak membership programs that feel like a tax on loyalty.

Let's look at how Rihanna's Savage X Fenty turned memberships into a wealth engine with 5M+ subscribers by stacking immediate and delayed benefits.

Here's the framework:

1. Instant Gratification Stack

- Major first purchase discount (TODAY only)
- Immediate store credit
- Extended refund window
- Free shipping from day one

2. Future Value Stack

- Annual gift (delayed reward)
- Milestone gift (after 5th month)
- Birthday gift (personalized timing)
- Skip any month (flexibility)

3. Exclusivity Stack

- Early access to drops
- Behind-the-scenes content
- Member-only discounts
- Brand partner perks

The secret sauce:

Personalized product curation based on purchase history + converting product sales into "credit" that never expires.

This removes the pressure of monthly products they might not want.

Pro Tip: Notice how the biggest discount is on day one - not delayed to the second purchase.

Most businesses get this backwards and wonder why conversion tanks.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Stack benefits. Stack profits.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The 4 Winning Strategies
emails.body:
Words I like: How to stay poor → Keep starting new things rather than getting good at one thing.

Mozi Minute: The Only 4 Winning Strategies

Most businesses try to be marginally better at everything and end up dominant at nothing.

There are only four competitive advantages that actually matter.

Pick one and you'll crush 99% of your competition.

Speed: Humans are wired to value instant results.

Compress the time between order and delivery. Create templates to eliminate decisions. Pre-make products/services. Increase availability.

Remember: Faster rarely costs more - it just requires smarter processes. Amazon didn't become Amazon by having better products. They won by getting those products to you faster than anyone else thought possible.

Risk: Customers will pay premium prices for certainty.

Focus on reliability, consistency, and smart guarantees.

Ever notice how people stick with the "safe" option even when cheaper alternatives exist? That's psychology working in your favor.

Reduce perceived risk and watch your sales grow.

Price: Being cheap only works if it's your entire business model.

Use AI, automation, and offshoring to slash costs while maintaining quality.

The winner isn't the company that charges less - it's the one that can profitably charge less while delivering the same value.

Think Walmart, not a flash sale.

Ease: Easy is what happens when everything that's hard is removed.

You don't actually achieve 'easy.' You make a list of everything that sucks, and eliminate the hardness one step at a time.

It's like good design…'it just works.'

You only need to dominate ONE of these areas to outperform almost everyone.

Pick your value vector. Build your processes. Craft your messaging.

Then watch as customers flock to the business that solves their problem in the specific way they value most.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Just win,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Three Stonecutters
emails.body:
Words I like: Culture is the spoken and unspoken rules that govern reward and punishment within an organization.

Mozi Minute: The Three Stone Cutters - Why Vision Makes You More

Three stonecutters were working side by side. A passerby asks them what they're doing.

First one's hunched over, replies:

"I'm cutting stone. Hate every second."

Second one's methodical, replies:

"Paying the bills. It's honest work."

Third one's different. Eyes bright, staying late:

"I'm building a cathedral that will stand for centuries. My grandchildren will see these spires pierce the heavens."

Same job. Same pay.

Wildly different results.

Companies obsess over "productivity hacks" and "performance management" but miss the biggest lever in business: vision.

That's what unlocks discretionary effort.

I define discretionary effort as work above the amount required to maintain employment.

This is the biggest unlockable resource in an organization.

It's the difference between being on a mission and clocking in / out.

You unlock discretionary effort with a clear vision and a culture that rewards excellence. Aka - talk about the cathedral, not the act of cutting stones.

"The difference between a job and a calling isn't the work - it's the worker's perspective." - Peter Drucker

This is why great cultures outperform great strategies.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Build cathedrals, not cubicles,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Start Stop Keep
emails.body:
Words I like: Most managers tell people who to be. Great managers tell people what to do.

Mozi Minute: Start Stop Keep Method

Every time I hear a manager say "be more proactive" or "show more leadership," I cringe.

Those words mean nothing.

Here's the framework that works:

Start. Stop. Keep.

Tell people exactly what actions you want.

No personality traits. No vague feedback. Pure action items.

Example:

Instead of "be more assertive," say "START speaking in the first 5 minutes of every meeting."

Instead of "be less of a dick," say "STOP interrupting people when they're presenting, take notes instead and ask if you can give feedback when they've finished."

The formula is simple:

- START doing [specific action]
- STOP doing [specific action]
- KEEP doing [specific action]

Make no mistake, it's harder to be specific.

But my mental hack for figuring out what I actually want them to do is to think of the *moment* I felt someone was lacking.

That *moment* came from an action they did or didn't take.

Then I just relay that back to them.

Ex: Why do I think Erica is lazy?

The *moment*: I messaged her last week and she didn't get back to me for 30min during work time and her calendar was empty.

→ Feedback: START: "I need you to turn on notifications and respond within 5min during working hours if not on a meeting."

That's it.

Fast clear feedback everyday until you retire. I wish I were kidding.

But that's the game. The only thing that changes, is who you give feedback to.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Clarity creates compliance,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Rush Fees (pls read)
emails.body:
Words I like: Urgency on their end means profit on yours.

Mozi Minute: Rush Fee/Speed Pass

Most businesses treat rush requests like a burden. Smart ones treat them like a profit center.

Your eyes should light up when a customer makes an unreasonable request. Because you get to counter with unreasonable pricing to match.

And oftentimes - they're happy to pay it!

On a personal note, I am almost always willing to pay 2-4x more to be first in line or prioritized.

Here's a sample pricing ladder:

- Triple Speed = Triple Price
- Double speed = Double price.
- Standard speed = Standard Price.

Translate it to the timelines that apply to your business. Post it everywhere.

The beauty?

You're not forcing speed - you're pricing choice.

Real World Examples:

Accountant: Tax return in 2 weeks = $500. One week = $750. 48 hours = $1,000.

Sign Shop: Banner in 5 days = $300. 2 days = $450. Today = $600.

Auto Shop: Standard wait = $200. Next day = $300. Within 4 hours = $400.

Don't apologize for these prices. Don't hide them.

Put them on your website. Some clients will always pay more for speed. Your job? Let them.

The profit math is simple:

If they want it faster, make it pricier.

Worst case? They pick normal speed & you make the same money.

Best case? They pay double for the exact same work you were gonna do anyways, you just do the work in a different order.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Speed costs more,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Wealth Alchemy (w/ Video)
emails.body:
Words I like: Most people optimize for income. The wealthy optimize for enterprise value.

Mozi Minute: Wealth Alchemy

Want to know how entrepreneurs become billionaires in a decade? (Bonus video below).

Two arbitrage plays that change everything.

- First: LTV to CAC.
- Second: CAC to Enterprise Value.

Once I understood the math, it changed how I did everything.

Here's how it works:

If you spend $10 to get a customer (CAC) who makes you $30 in annual EBITDA, and your company trades at 10x EBITDA, something magical happens.

That $10 spend instantly adds $300 to your company's value.

Think about that...

You're not making 2x or 3x your money. You're making 30x. Tax free.

But it gets better. You now have two choices with that $30 annual profit:

1) Take it out, pay taxes, keep $20

2) Reinvest it, get 3 more customers, add $900 to your company value (and by extension, you net worth…and here's the best part…tax free).

This is why the ultra-wealthy seem to get rich so fast.

They're not getting 10% annual returns.

They're getting 30x on every marketing dollar, then reinvesting every dollar of profit for another 30x.

Add zeros as desired to the prices I used, but the fundamentals remain.

But this only works once you have a company that checks the boxes of what makes a company sellable.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Happy hunting,

Alex

emails.subject: My AAA Close Framework
emails.body:
Most business owners hit a revenue ceiling they can't break through.

And after closing a lot of sales myself and now overseeing many many salespeople across our portfolio, I can tell you why:

Your sales ceiling IS your revenue ceiling.

Specifically, most deals are lost in the critical 3-second window after a prospect raises an objection.

Average companies ANSWER objections.

Scaling companies REFRAME them using the "3 A's Framework":

- ACKNOWLEDGE what the prospect said
- ASSOCIATE the objection with successful customer behavior
- ASK a question that maintains control

This framework is the cornerstone of how we train our portfolio's sales teams, and we want to help you implement it too.

Reframe. Close. Scale.

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Cost Centers to Cash Cows
emails.body:
Words I like: You can beat most people if you just stick with it for a year.

Mozi Minute: Cost Centers to Cash Cows

Most businesses have profit drivers hidden as expenses.

You're already paying for them. Your customers get value from them.

But you're not monetizing them.

After seeing this pattern repeat, I've found that almost ANY cost center can become a revenue generator with a simple mindset shift.

Real example:

I used to pay a water company for filters and new water to provide at my gym.

It cost a few hundred dollars a month - pure expense.

Later, I started selling water bottles and protein shakes by the door.

That water/protein station went from costing me money to generating almost enough to pay my rent.

The formula is simple:

Look at every expense in your business and ask:

"How can customers pay for this?"

and/or...

"Is there a better version of this thing I give for free, that they'd be willing to pay for?"

Don't just think about covering costs - think about creating profit centers.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Stop paying. Start profiting.

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Partner Scoring Acronym TEAM
emails.body:
Words I like: A good partner multiplies your strengths. A bad one multiplies your weaknesses.

Mozi Minute: TEAM Scoring Method

Most businesses try to find partners based on gut feel or surface-level chemistry.

They fail because people pick partners they like, instead of partners they need.

After 9 failed partnerships of my own, I've put together a framework that *might* increases your odds of success.

I call it T-E-A-M - and it's all about finding partners who have what you lack.

Here's how it works: T-E-A-M is an acronym

You want a partner who has…

Time:

Find someone who has the hours you don't.

If you're working IN the business, find someone who can work ON it.

If you handle ops, find someone who can do sales.

Expertise:

Partner with someone who knows what you don't.

If you're great at product but bad at marketing, find a marketing expert.

Don't double up on the same skills.

Assets:

Look for partners with resources you need - whether that's an email list, industry connections, or social following.

A friend of mine brought in a partner with a massive rolodex of brick & mortar distribution for his consumables.

Big win for everyone.

Money:

If you need capital, find someone who can invest.

But remember - money without the other three is just expensive funding.

The best money partners also bring Time, Expertise, or Assets.

Real example:

When I invested in Skool, I brought E-A-M. Expertise (marketing/business), Assets (my content & ads team infrastructure + audience + network + recruiting horsepower), and Money (self explanatory).

No matter what - if you're thinking through a partnership (of any size) I use these as my buckets for value creation.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Partner smart, scale fast,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: 5 Negotiation Tactics That Work
emails.body:
Words I like: The easiest way to lose in life is to try to avoid looking bad.

Mozi Minute:

5 Negotiation Tactics That Work

Most entrepreneurs suck at negotiating.

The problem is - you have to negotiate all the time.

You do it with vendors, employees, and obviously for deals.

If you didn't even think of those as negotiations, I can promise you're losing them.

After negotiating a lot of deals, here are the 5 tactics that actually work:

- BATNA (Best alternative to negotiated agreement):

The strongest position is having a great alternative if the deal fails.

Most people walk into negotiations with ONE option and wonder why they have no leverage.

Get multiple options lined up BEFORE negotiating.

The person who needs the deal less, wins.

Ex: If I'm talking to a vendor, I already want multiple offers in hand.

This makes it way more likely that I'm going to get a deal I like.

I can also get inspiration from the other vendor's terms to work out a perfect deal for me.

- Anchor First & Move Small:

Always set the first number to control the conversation.

If selling, anchor high. If buying, anchor low. When countering, use tiny increments.

Ex: If you offer $3M and they offer $5M. Your next move shouldn't be $4M, it should be $3.1M.

This signals you're not willing to move very much. You can even counter with the same price and a change in terms.

Make small concessions to get bigger ones.

- Multiple Equivalent Offers:

Never present ONE offer.

Give them 2-3 options you'd be happy with.

This forces them to reveal what they value while making you look flexible.

Later, you can mix-match the best parts for an even stronger deal.

Ex: Long commitment with lower fee and better service or short commitment with higher fee and slower service. Which would you prefer?

- Strategic Reciprocity:

Break deals into many variables (price, timing, risk, terms).

This creates more trading opportunities. Give small concessions to get bigger ones.

People feel compelled to return favors but rarely calculate their relative value.

Ex: If I throw away your trash at lunch then ask you to help me move.

It's one favor for one favor - but obviously I got the better deal.

All reciprocity is not created equal.

- Framing:

Never frame your price as a cost.

Frame it as an investment with returns.

Ex: A $100k pool isn't an expense - it's an asset that increases home value by $150k.

Suddenly your $100k price is actually a $50k gain.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Negotiate better, earn more,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The 90-Day Payment Lock-In
emails.body:
Words I like: The first three months determine the next three years.

Mozi Minute: The 90-Day Payment Lock-In

The first 90 days determine if you'll keep or lose a payment plan customer.

Yet most businesses treat day 90 the same as day 1. Big mistake.

Here's how to lock in more long-term customers using payment behavior data.

The data is clear:

After 3 successful payments, default risk drops by 35% (Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia).

But here's what most miss - it's not just about waiting it out.

It's about actively getting them through this window.

Here's your 90-day payment protection system:

1. Days 1-30: High-Touch Phase

- Welcome sequence focused on immediate value
- 48-hour check-in call
- Week 1 quick win celebration
- Payment reminder 3 days out

2. Days 31-60: Value Stacking

- Monthly value summary before payment
- Highlight one specific result they've gotten
- Preview next month's deliverables
- Payment reminder with success story

3. Days 61-90: Lock-In Phase

- Quarterly review call
- Document their progress/wins
- Share what's coming in months #4 through #6
- Payment reminder with their journey summary

Bottom Line:

Don't just remind them to pay. Remind them why they started paying in the first place.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Lock in the first 90, bank the next 900,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Onboarding = Profit Center
emails.body:
Words I like: If you can be in a bad mood for no reason, you might as well be in a good mood for no reason.

Mozi Minute: Onboarding = Profit Center

Most businesses treat customer onboarding as a cost center.

You pay someone to hold hands, answer questions, and get customers set up.

But smart businesses turn these exact same interactions into profit centers through affiliate deals.

I'll give you an example, the math, and implementation steps...

Here's a real world example:

I used to pay one girl to onboard all new customers. It worked but it cost me money.

So, I made a small change. Instead of just doing the normal script, she'd also make sure customers clicked our affiliate links for every tool, product, and service they would need during that call.

Real fast, the affiliate revenue completely paid for her position.

Not long after, it started to pay for the expanded team as we scaled.

That "cost center" hilariously became a profit center.

Hypothetical Math:

Let's say you have 3 tools you use and you get a 20% commission from them.

- If tool one is $200/mo.
- Tool 2 is $400/mo, and..
- Tool 3 is a one time $1000 purchase.

By simply adding this process in, you'd get $320 up front (which more than covers the cost of labor for the call), then $120/mo after that.

After you onboard 40 or so customers (only 10 a week), that entire position would be paid for.

Implementation: You're doing the work, might as well get paid for it.

- List every product/tool your customers need
- Sign up for those affiliate programs
- Create a "resources" document with YOUR links
- Train your onboarding team to reference it
- Track the revenue (you'll be shocked)

As promised, money in under a minute.

Keep crushing,

- Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Pick One, Get Both
emails.body:
Words I like: Customers forget what you promised, but they never forget how you made them feel.

Mozi Minute: Pick One, Get Both

Most businesses either give no signup bonus or just hand it over without fanfare.

But customers remember the peak emotional experience and the end.

This is called Peak-End bias.

To harness this in the real world, try this:

The "Pick One, Get Both" surprise.

Here's exactly how it works.. right after they sign up, say:

"As a welcome gift, you can choose either [Bonus A] or [Bonus B] - which would you prefer?"

Let them pick.

Then hit them with:

"You know what? Since you're awesome, how about I just give you both."

For example:

"Would you rather have our recipe book or our workout tank top? [pause] Actually, take both!"

Why this works:

- Choice creates engagement (they mentally commit to one)
- Surprise creates delight (they get both)
- Timing is perfect (right when buyer's remorse typically kicks in)
- Cost is the same (you were gonna give something anyway)
- They appreciate whichever one they didn't choose because the moment you present them and tell them they have to choose... they become deprived of one.

And as soon as you deliver, you relieve them of it. This is gratitude operationalized.

Pro tip:

Split any existing bonus you have into two parts.

Even a digital product can be split into "Part 1" and "Part 2" - then surprise them with both.

No extra cost, pure profit.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Surprise them. Delight them. Retain them.

- Alex

emails.subject: Feeling stuck? Here's why.
emails.body:
It's not sales or marketing, it's actually...

Most business owners feel stuck and assume it's due to sales, marketing, or team issues.

They push harder, spend more, but nothing moves the needle.

Here's why:

Every business has ONE constraint at any given time.

Solve it, and growth happens. Work on literally anything else…and it doesn't.

Sounds easy. But growth hardly is. So here's the catch:

Most entrepreneurs can't see their real bottleneck… they're just too close to it.

- Your "sales" issue might actually be a recruiting or onboarding problem.
- The "marketing" struggle might be pricing, positioning, or even the product itself.
- The "team" problem is often a missing system or clarity around roles.

Find your constraint. Fix it. Scale.

- Alex Hormozi

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Time-Based Surge Pricing
emails.body:
Words I like: If you're charging the same amount during your busy times as your empty times, you're losing money.

Mozi Minute: Time-Based Pricing

Most businesses have surge hours but not surge prices. Makes no sense. And yes, what I'm about to share works with normal everyday businesses.

If Saturday at 7pm is packed, but Tuesday at 3pm is dead, why are your prices identical?

Here's the fix: Add time-based pricing.

Ex 1:

Gyms charge $49/mo but are slammed 5-7pm?

Make it $39/mo for morning access, $69/mo for peak hours.

Ex 2:

Restaurant packed every weekend?

Have a weekend menu that's 10% higher.

Ex 3:

Hair salon booked solid every Friday-Saturday?

Make Friday-Saturday cuts cost 20% more than Sun-Thurs.

The math is simple:

If people are fighting for spots during peak times, those spots are underpriced.

And if you're afraid to make the change - just remember:

Worst case? You change it back.

Best case? You make more money doing exactly what you're already doing - which is my favorite way to make money.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Price to your peaks,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The "One-Question Close"
emails.body:
Words I like: Sales isn't about having the perfect pitch - it's about asking the right question at the right time.

Mozi Minute: The "One-Question Close"

Most sales calls end with a weak close. You pitch, the prospect hesitates, and you start fumbling with discounts or "just following up" emails.

Instead of chasing, try this:

Right after you finish presenting your offer, ask:

"Based on what we discussed, do you feel like this solves your problem?"

Then, shut up (reminder that Columbia saw a 32% increase in close rates when sales people waited over 8 seconds after asking for the sale…so…hush).

Why this works:

- It forces commitment. If they say "yes," you move straight to closing. If they say "no," they show their real objection.

- It eliminates sales pressure. Instead of pushing, you're letting them come to their own conclusion.

- It makes rejection awkward. If they just admitted it solves their problem, what reason do they have to say no?

Give it a shot. It's the simplest close you'll ever use—and one of the most effective.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Want more sales and scaling tactics? We just opened spots for our next Scaling Workshop in Vegas. We turn away 70% of applicants to keep the room quality high.

Say less. Close more.

Alex

PS: unrelated funny meme that made me laugh out loud with Leila. Maybe this is you with your operator (or whoever actually does the work in your business).

emails.subject: Are You Solving The Wrong Constraint? [Mozi Minute]
emails.body:
Words I like: Many entrepreneurs try to fix demand when they actually have a supply problem. Figure out which constraint is real before you throw money at marketing.

Mozi Minute: Supply vs Demand Constraints

I see businesses waste countless dollars (and time) solving the wrong problem. They dump money into ads when they can't handle more customers, or obsess over operations when no one's buying.

Here's how to diagnose which constraint you actually have and what to do about it.

Your business is either supply constrained or demand constrained.

Never both at the same time.

Demand constrained = You have capacity but not enough customers.

Supply constrained = You have customers but not enough capacity.

Here's the test: If you doubled your marketing budget tomorrow, would you double your business or create a nightmare?

If nightmare → You're supply constrained.

If doubled business → You're demand constrained.

Real world examples:

- Restaurant with empty tables: Demand constrained (fix your marketing)
- Restaurant with 2-hour wait time: Supply constrained (fix operations to move faster, add take out, raise prices, or expand)
- Consultant fully booked 3 months out: Supply constrained (raise prices by at least 50%)
- Consultant with open calendar slots next week: Demand constrained (get more leads)

Bottom Line: Businesses that are supply constrained often think they need more customers. They don't.

They need more margin, more capacity, or both. Fix with higher prices, more operating leverage (better client to provider ratio/tech to speed up delivery), or recruiting to expand capacity.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Fix the real problem, unlock real growth.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The “Live Demo” Pitch
emails.body:
Words I like: People will believe your future the moment you show it to them.

Mozi Minute: The "Live Demo" Pitch

Most businesses try to close sales with big promises of future results...

But we found something far more powerful: giving prospects a tiny taste of the actual experience before asking for the sale.

Here's how it works:

Instead of just describing what you'll do, do a small piece of it right there on the call.

Real Example we did with a marketing agency about 9 months ago:

- Old way: "We can get you 100 qualified leads a month."

- New way: "Mind if I play you a live call that's happening right now with a lead we generated for a plumber in your area…*plays call* yea, this is happening right now. Do you think you could handle 100 of these a month if we sent them to you?"

Watch the glint in their eyes. This will beat any pitch you can come up with.

More examples:

- Gym: Do one perfect form correction

- Marketing: Fix one headline live

- Consulting: Solve one specific problem

- Software: Show one premium feature live

The key: It must be

- Fast (under 60 seconds)
- Valuable (obvious benefit)
- Easy to understand
- Impossible to fake

Tiny proof beats a big promise every time.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Show n' sell,

Alex

emails.subject: Private video from Alex
emails.body:

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: What Business Are You REALLY In?
emails.body:
Words I like: What got you from zero to a million isn't what's going to get you from 10 to 30 million.

The biggest opportunity is the one right in front of you.

The vast majority of businesses are stuck because they're working on the wrong stuff.

Entrepreneurs get comfortable solving problems they know how to solve, then wonder why they plateau.

Here's why: you're probably in a completely different business than you think.

When I got into gyms, I thought I was in the fitness business. I wasn't. I was in the marketing and sales business.

When I got into supplements, I thought I was in the product business. I wasn't. I was in the brand, media, and distribution business.

When I got into software, I thought I was in the marketing and sales business. I wasn't. I was in the product business.

Real world: Every industry has its "big hairy problem" - the thing that actually determines success.

A gym owner friend started a cleaning business thinking it was like gyms (marketing and sales).

Turns out, cleaning is a recruiting and training business.

Once he flipped his mindset, he went from $40k to $150k in monthly revenue within a year.

How to find your real business:

- Ask successful people in your industry: "What's the hardest part of your business?"

- Look at the biggest companies in your space - what are they exceptional at?

- Identify your constraint - what's keeping you from doubling revenue?

The moment you identify what business you're REALLY in, everything changes.

You stop wasting time on distractions (what I call the "woman in the red dress") and focus on breaking through the concrete wall in front of you.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Solve the right problem, scale the right business.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Competitor Callback
emails.body:
Words I like: Curiosity is stronger than desire when it comes to getting someone's attention.

Mozi Minute: The Competitor Callback Hack

I'm about to share the single most effective voicemail script I've ever found. And it's only 17 words long. After thousands of voicemails left over 15 years, nothing else comes close to its callback rate.

Here's the exact script:

"Hi, this is [your name] calling in regards to [their competitor]. Give me a call back when you get a chance."

Example salesperson calling McDonald's:

"Hi this is John, I'm calling in regards to Burger King. Please give me a call back when you get a chance."

That's it. No explanation. No urgency. No value prop.

Why it works:

1. Creates instant curiosity (are you working with their competitor?)

2. Triggers competitive anxiety (what's happening with their competitor?)

3. Feels important but not salesy

4. Short enough that they actually listen to it all

Key points:

- Name their DIRECT competitor (someone they actually worry about)

- Keep your tone casual and matter-of-fact

- Don't add anything else to the message

- Be ready with your actual pitch when they call back "Yes, Burger King is killing you on search traffic, I was wondering if you wanted to match or exceed their traffic within the next 90 days"

Side Note: Only use this if you have something valuable to say when they call back. This isn't a gimmick - it's an opener that gets you the conversation you actually need to have.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Get to the point, get to the profit,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Triple Review Merch Hack
emails.body:
Words I like: If you can be in a bad mood for no reason, you might as well be in a good mood for no reason.

Mozi Minute: Triple Review Merch Hack

Most businesses beg for reviews or hope customers leave them naturally. Here's what actually worked for me when launching new locations: exclusive merch in exchange for triple-platform reviews.

Here's exactly what I did:

I ordered t-shirts for $5-6 each (get the nice ones). I put a cool design on it and put my logo on the back or sleeve. I asked people who wanted one for free in my online community. A ton of people did. Then I told them they were not for sale and ONLY available for people who left us an honest review on all 3 platforms (Google, Yelp, and Facebook).

The math was simple:

Cost: $5 per shirt

Return: 3 genuine reviews ($1.67 per review)

Extra benefit: Walking billboards

Key execution points:

- Make the shirts actually look good & good quality (people won't wear ugly freebies)
- Show them exactly how to leave reviews (screenshot instructions)
- Don't ask for 5-star reviews. Ask for honest reviews. Less cringe. (They'll still all be good).
- Have them show you the reviews to get the shirt
- Give the shirt immediately - no delays
- BONUS - ask them if they want you to take a picture with it on or at the facility and post it.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Make your marketing wearable,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Silence Creates A 32% Boost in Closing
emails.body:
Words I like: In sales, the person who talks the first after price, loses. Sometimes the best thing to say is nothing.

Mozi Minute: The Silent Close Technique

So I stumbled on this bit of research that confirmed a long held belief of mine.

- Columbia Business School found purchases increase by 32% when salespeople extend that silence to 8+ seconds.
- Yet, research from the University of Texas shows the average salesperson waits only 2.7 seconds after asking for the sale before jumping back in.

Hence, the problem.

Theory:

Your prospect's brain needs 6-8 seconds to process a (large) decision. When you talk during that time, you interrupt their buying process. It's like hitting reset on their mental math (and increase the time to buy).

Practice:

- Ask for the sale clearly
- Shut up and count to 8 (silently)
- ...then count to 8 again

We implemented this with a sales team last month. We got a small bump in sales immediately - mainly from the guys who weren't already doing this. To be clear. The jump didn't come from changing the pitch, just from changing the pause length after asking for the sale. And the best part, there was no new training/scripting besides "count to 8 after you make the ask."

Pro tip: 8 seconds is easy to say but feels like an eternity in reality. Look at the call time on your phone or dialer to actually make sure you wait out the full length. After all, 32% higher close rates is nothing to sneeze at.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Talk less, sell more,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Better Voicemail Makes You More
emails.body:
Words I like: The fastest way to make more money is to find the conversations that never happened.

Mozi Minute: Better Voicemail Makes You More

Most businesses lose 62% of their inbound calls to voicemail (CallRail study). But here's what's crazy - the standard "we'll call you back" message costs you 89% of those potential customers.

Here's a simple voicemail change that boosts callbacks & pick ups.

Here's the exact script:

"Hi! You've reached [business]. We're with a customer but if you leave your name and number, [specific person's name] will personally call you back within 15 minutes during business hours. And because we made you wait, we'll give you [specific valuable offer] just for connecting with us. Leave your message at the beep!"

Why it works:

- Names a specific person (creates accountability)
- Gives exact callback time (removes uncertainty)
- Offers instant value for the inconvenience
- Makes a promise you can track

Real example:

Gym changed from generic voicemail to:

"Hi! You've reached Elite Fitness. We're training a client but Sarah will personally call you back within 15 minutes. Leave your info and we'll give you two free sessions just for connecting with us."

Cost to implement: $0

Time to implement: 5 minutes

As promised, money in under a minute.

Change the message, bank the difference,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: $97 beats $47 Front End Offer
emails.body:
Words I like: If you want premium customers, stop selling cheap stuff.

Mozi Minute: $97 beats $47

Most people think that charging something vs giving something away for free brings in a higher quality customer. And, that's true...but you can actually take it a step further by testing the low ticket prices. We just got fascinating data from a split test for one of our portfolio companies that made us a lot of money.

Here's what we tested:

Two identical sales funnels

Only difference: Low ticket price

- Group A: $49

- Group B: $97

The LTV changes over time between the two groups shocked us:

LTV By Day 0: $51.42 (exactly what you'd expect $97 - $49 = $52)

LTV By Day 30: $212.60 difference (!)

LTV By Day 120: $292.60 difference (!!)

LTV By Day 180: $891 difference (!!!)

Translation: The $97 buyers spent nearly $900 more per person over 180 days. And here's the kicker - customer acquisition cost was nearly identical (within 15%).

The hidden gold: Higher front-end prices didn't just make more money upfront - they positioned the product better in customers minds to get the same customers to be willing to pay more AND attracted better buyers. We're talking 41% higher lifetime value at 180 days...from ONE change.

My takeaway: Your front-end price isn't just a revenue decision - it's a customer quality filter.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Test prices, bank profits,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: 67% Increase In Retention
emails.body:
Words I like: People don't leave companies, they leave conversations that never happened.

Mozi Minute: 67% Increase in Retention

Every business wants to make more money. But so few look at the largest profit centers - retention.

Businesses lose out on mountains of cash by letting customers 'idle.' They float around in no man's land, get impatient, then leave to find someone who can help them NOW.

Fun fact: Customers who have their problems resolved in under 5 minutes are 67% more likely to stick around (Harvard study). And yet, most businesses have multi-day response times. No bueno.

Here's how I like to speed up response times.

First, map every possible customer issue into three buckets:

- Can solve immediately (ex: password resets, basic questions)
- Need more info (ex: technical issues, account specific)
- Need approval (ex: refunds, special requests)

Then, create three corresponding response templates:

Bucket 1: Direct solution (template these)

Bucket 2: Specific questions to get info (template the questions that get the right response)

Bucket 3: "I'm escalating this to [name] who can approve this. They'll reach out within [time]."

The key: Respond to EVERY message within 5 minutes, even if just to acknowledge and set expectations. Kinda like airplane delays. They just keep telling you every 30min that it's delayed. People just like to be kept abreast, even if the solution isn't fully formed yet.

Train your team to respond fast with something rather than slow with everything. Speed beats perfection when it comes to customer satisfaction.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Fast replies save lives,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Contrast Closing
emails.body:
Words I like: Nothing is ever expensive in isolation, only in relation. But the key to sales is comparing your price to the right other number.

Mozi Minute: The Contrast Close

They've proven that people make decisions based on relative value, not absolute value. Yet most businesses present prices in isolation. Here's how to use contrast to make your prices feel smaller.

The framework:

- Start with the cost of their problem (yearly)

Ex: Customer losing $300k/year on bad ad spend
- Break that into monthly cost

Ex: That's $25k/month in waste
- Present your solution cost (monthly)

Ex: Our service: $5k/month
- Show the contrast ratio

Contrast ratio: Every $1 spent saves $5

TLDR: Anchor them high with their problem cost first. Then hit them with the contrast. Frame it as a black and white return.

Pro tip: People would rather avoid loss than acquire gain. Framing solutions to make known problems go away can be more compelling (and believable) than anchoring on unknown upside. If you're struggling to close, try this and you may get more sales across the line.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Frame first, close more,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Gift-Review Loop
emails.body:
Words I like: People help people before they help businesses.

Mozi Minute: The Gift-Review Loop

Most businesses make the mistake of having owners ask for reviews directly. But people don't feel connected to owners - they feel connected to the employees who helped them. Here's how to use that psychology to get more authentic reviews.

Have your employees surprise customers with an unexpected gift or extra service. Then, as the owner, send a personal message: "Hey [name], [employee] told me how much they enjoyed [serving] you. They get a [$50] bonus if you leave them a 5-star review sharing your experience. Would you mind taking 2 minutes to help them out?"

Why this works:

- Unexpected gifts create reciprocity
- Customer feels they're helping the employee, not the business
- The request comes after value is already given
- Clear, specific ask with a concrete benefit to someone they know

As promised, money in under a minute.

Want more real world tactics that drive revenue? We just opened spots for our next Scaling Workshop in Vegas on April 7/8th.

Help them help you,

Alex

PS - Your employee's face when they realize being extra generous just earned them another bonus...

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Money Making Clarity Test
emails.body:
Words I like: If you can't explain why you believe what you believe, it's not your belief, it's someone else's.

Mozi Minute: Money Making Clarity Test

I've found a simple way to identify if your team actually understands how they drive revenue: Ask them to explain it. Sounds obvious, but most can't. Here's what happens when you ask "How does what you do make the company money?": blank stares, vague answers about "helping the team", or my favorite "I'm on a lot of meetings."

Here's what works:

Have every employee write down their answer. Not in a group. Not verbally. Written. Then, have their direct manager review it. If they can't clearly explain how their work connects to revenue in one paragraph, you have a problem.

Real example of a good answer from customer support: "When I solve customer problems quickly, they stay subscribed longer. Each month they stay is more revenue. Plus, happy customers leave good reviews which helps sales close more deals."

Bad answer: "I help the team by answering customer questions and solving their issues."

Two immediate benefits:

- You identify which employees don't understand their revenue impact
- You learn which managers haven't explained it well

Fix this by having managers work 1-on-1 with their direct reports until everyone can articulate their money-making impact. When people can explain how they drive revenue, it makes it more likely they actually do it.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Want help building a team that understands how to drive growth? We just opened spots for our next Scaling Workshop in Vegas on April the 7/8th.

Make the connection clear,

Alex

PS - what it looks like when your team gets money clarity

emails.subject: Mozi Money Minute: Solve for compounding
emails.body:
Words I Like: If you want better friends, get used to seeing them less. Better friends are busy friends.

Mozi Money Minute: Solve For Compounding

As soon as you learn how to market and sell, you can become a millionaire, even a decamillionaire. But to become a centimillionaire and beyond, you need something else. You need to have all that work marketing and selling stick. You need it to stack. So that the size of your company is relative to the sales you do over a decade, not just what you sold last month.

There are only two ways to do this:

- Make something people never stop buying: either a subscription (Netflix) recurring revenue, or something people come back to again and again on a predictable basis (Coca-cola) - aka re-occurring revenue.

- Build a network of people who never stop selling. Think real estate brokerages. The people buying houses doesn't recur per se, but recruiting agents who regularly sell X houses per month is the compounding component of the business. If you have a physical product, all your nodes of distribution (ie. affiliates or retail stores) become the people that stack over time.

You need to solve for one. If you don't, you'll be stuck every month having to live hand to mouth. And you'll always be one bad advertising campaign or domain ban away from losing everything. No fun.

"Sometimes you gotta go slow to go fast." It would be better to spend two years finding something that sticks than spend two years growing revenue through marketing, only to hit a plateau you can't breakthrough.

Otherwise, slay the day.

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Ad Spend Wall
emails.body:
Words I like: If your business isn't growing, you're probably being efficient at the wrong things.

Mozi Minute: The Ad Spend Wall

Most businesses hit a wall with their ad spend and blame the platform, the algorithm, or their audience size. But after analyzing A LOT of campaigns that hit this ceiling, we found three consistent bottlenecks. Once you unlock them, I've seen companies go from $1,000 per day in ad spend To $10,000 per day and even $100,000 per day. They are that significant.

Here's what really stops your ads from scaling:

- Mediocre Creative

Your ads aren't scroll-stopping enough. Period. And no, running 5 variations isn't enough. We found businesses that make 10x more creative (yes, literally 10x) break through plateaus consistently. This is also supported by Meta's data saying the top 1% of advertisers make 11x more creative.

- Weak Free Offer

Your lead magnet probably sucks. Harsh, but true. The businesses that scale past $100k/month in ad spend all have one thing in common: They give away what others charge for. If your free thing isn't better than your competitor's paid thing, you'll stay stuck. Solution: Give away 10x more value. Make it absurd.

- Insufficient Backend LTV

You might be "efficient" at $5k/day in spend, but can't scale past it. That's not a traffic problem - it's a lifetime value problem. Your backend monetization isn't strong enough to support higher CPAs. A few years ago, one of our portfolio companies was "stuck" at $25,000 per day in ad spend. We doubled LTV and we were able to scale to $150,000 per day in ad spend (not a typo). If you can spend more, an ocean of traffic opens up to you.

Bottom line: You can be the most efficient advertiser in the world, but with weak creative, a cheap free offer, and low LTV, you're just being efficient at staying small.

As promised, value in under a minute.

Fix the bottlenecks, feed the growth,

Alex

PS - Your media buyer's face when you tell them they can spend twice as much to get leads. (This one is probably one of my top 10 sent memes of all time)

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The 4R Customer Journey
emails.body:
Words I like: The only thing crazier than chasing your goals is expecting other people to understand them.

Mozi Minute: The 4R Customer Journey

Most businesses get customers. Some of those customers refer, others stick around, some leave reviews, some buy more stuff. And all of those are good things. And most times, those good things happen on accident. I've found I get significantly more customers to do what I want if I design it on purpose.

Here's how I do it:

I use a simple customer journey system. It's easy to remember. It's 4 Rs: Retain, Review, Refer, Resell. Most businesses get maybe one of these. Here's how to get all four:

Retain: Increase the likelihood they stay by clearly mapping out exactly what happens in days 1-30-90. Show them the finish line before they start running. Your customers can't hit a target they can't see.

Review: Make it simple. Send them a review link 72 hours after their first win (not 72 hours after purchase). Include a before/after template they can fill in. Ex: "Before working with [company], I struggled with X. After working with them, I achieved Y in just Z time." People respond to structure.

Refer: Give them a reason AND a reminder. Most businesses ask once, then quit. Instead, create a "Referral Moment" - the exact time when customers are most likely to refer. For a gym, it's when they hit their first goal. For software, it's after their first successful export. Whatever it is - systematize then incentivize.

Resell: The easiest sale you'll ever make is to someone who already bought. But timing is everything. The key: move your upsell from an arbitrary timeline to the moment of greatest need for the new solution. Sometimes it's immediate. Sometimes it's at a delay. It just depends on what you sell and when they are most aware of the problem that needs solving. Offer your upsell/resell then.

At the very least, plan SOMETHING to happen for each of the 4Rs and you'll increase the likelihood they happen.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Build the system, bank the value,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The 3-7-30 Method
emails.body:
Words I like: The person who asks the most times gets the most. The problem is, people don't like being asked. So you have to give value consistently so you can earn the right to keep asking. Winners win because they take more shots on goal. But they only get those shots on goal because they know how to get into scoring position.

Mozi Money Minute: The 3-7-30 Method

Most businesses get leads. Then, follow up 1-2 times. Then, stop. But the majority of customers don't buy that fast. Meaning, by only following up 1-2 times, you are losing probably half of your sales or more. People just need more information to make a purchase. But, most businesses don't know how to follow up without saying "just buy my thing" over and over again. No bueno. You can fix this 'not knowing how to follow up' issue with a simple framework I call "The 3-7-30 Method."

Here's how it works:

First 3 Days: Share your best case study day 1, best client transformation day 2, and biggest industry insight day 3.

Next 7 Days: One touch every other day focused on removing specific objections. Each email tackles ONE fear/concern your prospects typically have. Think of this as a reverse FAQ with proof woven in.

Next 30 Days: Two touches per week alternating between success stories and industry insights. The key: make each valuable enough to stand alone. If you are reading this email for example, you are here.

Note: Don't think "follow-up." Think "value sequence." Every touch should make them think "Damn, this is good stuff even if I don't buy."

Real example:

We implemented this with a portfolio company and changed their follow up from 3 emails to using the 3-7-30 method. Result: Lead conversion doubled...yea.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Don't forget to follow up,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Premium-Only Menu
emails.body:
Words I like: If you want better clients, stop selling cheap stuff.

Words I like too: If you want better customers, stop offering the path of least investment.

Mozi Minute: The Premium-Only Menu

Most service businesses kill their profit by offering a "budget tier" to capture more clients. But here's what actually happens: cheap clients choose it AND premium clients choose it (because why wouldn't they?). You end up with a business full of your worst offering.

Here's the fix: Remove ALL budget options. Only show 3 premium choices. Pro Tip: Don't inch up gradually. Make a clean break to premium-only options.

Example marketing agency:

Old menu:

- Basic: $1,000/mo
- Standard: $2,000/mo
- Premium: $3,000/mo
- Elite: $5,000/mo

New menu:

- Advanced: $3,000/mo
- Premium: $5,000/mo
- Elite: $8,000/mo

The best part:

- Budget clients disqualify themselves before wasting sales time
- Quality prospects show up ready to invest

As promised, money in under a minute.

Raise floors, raise profits,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The Daily Cost Close
emails.body:
Words I like: People who say "it's too expensive" have never done the math on staying poor.

Mozi Minute: The Daily Cost Close

Most salespeople freeze when they hear "that's too expensive" - then fumble into discounts or feature comparisons. Lame. There's a better way to downsell without discounting.

Harvard Business School Professor John Gourville's research figured out people are significantly more likely to buy when prices are broken into smaller daily amounts versus larger payments less often. It was called the "Pennies-a-Day" study if you're curious.

Why it works: People judge daily amounts against daily purchases (coffee, lunch) but judge monthly amounts against big expenses (rent, car). You want them comparing your solution to coffee, not their mortgage.

When to use it: (NOT AS AN OPENING STRATEGY!)

- Already hit a price objection
- Selling to budget-conscious customers
- Need to overcome sticker shock
- Making a final close

Here's how to use this hack: First, say your price is $1200 for 6 months. This anchors the number. Then, if they say no, break it into absurdly small daily amounts. It's like a pseudo price drop.

For example:

"Our executive program is $12,000 per year... [if they object]

Look at it this way - that's just $33 per day to solve this problem. You're spending more than that on employee turnover right now..."

And here's what makes this lethal: After showing the daily cost, hit them with. "Would you say [Starbucks/lunch/etc.] is worth more to you than getting unstuck?"

As promised, money in under a minute.

Break it down, close it up,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: Sign “In the way” Hack
emails.body:
Words I like: The best marketing is the kind that works.

Mozi Minute: Sign "In the way" Hack

I wasted a lot of time and money designing print signs and buying digital ones. Most customers completely ignored them. Wanna know what actually worked?

A simple A-frame sign with handwriting placed in customers' walking path.

Two ways I used A-Frame signs to make money:

How most people do it: At my locations with heavier foot traffic, I put it on the sidewalk with a crazy offer to try and get people to walk inside. That worked okay - but not great. I'd get a few sales a month, but nothing to write home about.

What worked for me: What REALLY worked well was making it my internal promotions board. I'd rotate between 'gives' and 'asks.' This way you train customers to want to read it and follow instructions. If it's always promotions, people learn to ignore it quickly. Think of it like an "in person" email.

Week 1: (Give) Joke of the day - silly - but people read it.

Week 2: (Give) Extra credit for some tiny nugget ("Get a free t shirt if you leave us a review")

LEAD WITH THE THING THEY GET, NOT WHAT THEY DO FOR IT, IMPORTANT!

Week 3: (Ask) Special Offer

Week 4: (Give) Ask the front desk for a goodie

Places you can put them: 1) outside your door 2) right inside entrance, 3) blocking footpath of best selling products/high usage area.

Here's why it works:

1) Physical interruption: Place it where customers HAVE to walk around it. Don't be polite with placement. Make them notice it.

2) Handwritten = authentic: Use actual handwriting (even if messy). We tested this against professional signs. The handwritten versions converted 2.3x better.

3) Daily change: Rotate the message every day. Even if it's the same deal. Having that actual day's date hits different.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Make them stop, make more money,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: The 20 Year Master
emails.body:
Words I like: The reason it's taking so long is because you're in a rush.

Other Words I like: It's not happening as fast as you want because you're not as good as you think you are.

Mozi Minute: The 20 Year Master

A young samurai once asked a Zen master how long it would take to achieve mastery. "10 years," the master said.

The samurai, eager to speed things up, asked "What if I work twice as hard and practice day and night?"

The master replied: "20 years."

When the confused samurai asked why it would take longer, the master explained: "If you have one eye fixed on your destination, you only have one eye left to find your way."

I see this in business every week.

The entrepreneurs most obsessed with "scaling fast" are usually the ones who scale slowest. Why?

They skip the foundations. They don't build systems. They chase shortcuts.

We had a portfolio company trying to hit $10M. Kept missing targets. Finally slowed down. Built proper systems. Fixed their operations. Revenue doubled in 6 months.

Sometimes you have to slow down to speed up.

"You can't have a baby in a month by getting 9 women pregnant" - Warren Buffett

Great feats take time. And that's why so few people accomplish them.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Build slow, grow fast,

Alex

emails.subject: Mozi Minute: 80% Gross Margin Rule of Thumb
emails.body:
Words I like: The best way to hit next year's goals is to not wait until next year to work on them.

Mozi Money Minute: 80% Gross Margins on Services

Too many businesses charge too little for their services. They're surprised when at the end of the month they have nothing left. And this shouldn't be a surprise. They typically look around at what everyone else charges, and offer a little more, for a little less. This is silly. 1) Because you should charge off value, not your competitors. 2) Most of your competition is broke. So don't copy them.

My Rule of Thumb: I want gross margins to be >80% (*exception for companies that sell recurring labor by the hour - think security guard services - different monster). This means if I charge $1000 per month, I want my all in cost of delivery to be LESS THAN $200. This starts me at 80% margins for my business, then I have to pay for everything else to eek out my profit. Now, if I start there, then I keep attempting to over deliver, and expand my margin as we get better. This is how you print money. It's how we've kept over 50%+ net margins in every cash flow business I've founded since the turn of the decade.

Instead of immediately thinking "this wont work for my industry" ask yourself "how could i make this work for my industry?"

In my next email - I'm going to follow up on another gross margin misconception you won't want to miss. It's especially relevant to those of you who want to scale to big #s.

As promised, money in under a minute.

Margins matter,

Alex

Managing partner Acquisition.com
